`MysqlInnoDbMvccSnapshotIT#shouldUseReadLockMvccWhenDoingSnapshot` fails with
```
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7428530Z  but was: SourceRecord{sourcePartition={server=simulation}, sourceOffset={ts_sec=1780030795, file=mysql-bin.000004, pos=470028}} ConnectRecord{topic='simulation.innodb_mvcc_1f4enqk.test_table', kafkaPartition=null, key=Struct{id=101}, keySchema=Schema{simulation.innodb_mvcc_1f4enqk.test_table.Key:STRUCT}, value=Struct{after=Struct{id=101,name=John Doe1000,email=johndoe@gmail.com,last_updated=2026-05-29T04:59:55Z},source=Struct{version=3.6.0-SNAPSHOT,connector=mysql,name=simulation,ts_ms=1780030795000,snapshot=last,db=innodb_mvcc_1f4enqk,ts_us=1780030795000000,ts_ns=1780030795000000000,table=test_table,server_id=0,file=mysql-bin.000004,pos=470028,row=0},op=r,ts_ms=1780030795457,ts_us=1780030795457622,ts_ns=1780030795457622311}, valueSchema=Schema{simulation.innodb_mvcc_1f4enqk.test_table.Envelope:STRUCT}, timestamp=null, headers=ConnectHeaders(headers=[ConnectHeader(key=__debezium.context.connectorLogicalName, value=simulation, schema=Schema{STRING}), ConnectHeader(key=__debezium.context.taskId, value=0, schema=Schema{STRING}), ConnectHeader(key=__debezium.context.connectorName, value=mysql, schema=Schema{STRING}), ConnectHeader(key=__debezium.context.runId, value=019e721a-6e30-7333-ad0f-b77df8a70fe1, schema=Schema{STRING})])} within 30 seconds.
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7437716Z 	at org.testcontainers.shaded.org.awaitility.core.ConditionAwaiter.await(ConditionAwaiter.java:167)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7438967Z 	at org.testcontainers.shaded.org.awaitility.core.AssertionCondition.await(AssertionCondition.java:119)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7440224Z 	at org.testcontainers.shaded.org.awaitility.core.AssertionCondition.await(AssertionCondition.java:31)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7441551Z 	at org.testcontainers.shaded.org.awaitility.core.ConditionFactory.until(ConditionFactory.java:1160)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7443008Z 	at org.testcontainers.shaded.org.awaitility.core.ConditionFactory.untilAsserted(ConditionFactory.java:790)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7444452Z 	at io.debezium.connector.binlog.InnoDBMvccSnapshot.shouldUseReadLockMvccWhenDoingSnapshot(InnoDBMvccSnapshot.java:83)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7445845Z 	at java.base/java.lang.reflect.Method.invoke(Method.java:580)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7446529Z 	at java.base/java.util.ArrayList.forEach(ArrayList.java:1596)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7447153Z 	at java.base/java.util.ArrayList.forEach(ArrayList.java:1596)
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7447680Z Caused by: org.opentest4j.AssertionFailedError: 
2026-05-29T05:00:25.7448110Z expected: null
```

This is very caused by https://github.com/debezium/debezium/pull/7473 which besides disabling auto-commit also introduced commit in `BinlogConnectorConnection#getEstimatedTableSize`. This may result into broken consistency of the snapshot transaction is committed during snapshot and subsequent select may see newly committed data. This is also demonstrated by failing tests above.

title: Cassandra fails to start with a template NullPointerException
body:
# Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

Debezium Server (`quay.io/debezium/server:3.6.0.Alpha2`) with the Cassandra 4 source connector (`io.debezium.connector.cassandra.Cassandra4Connector`, `EXTRA_CONNECTOR: cassandra-4`).

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

```
connector.class                              = io.debezium.connector.cassandra.Cassandra4Connector
topic.prefix                                 = cassandra
cassandra.node.id                            = docker-05-cdc
cassandra.hosts                              = cassandra
cassandra.port                               = 9042
cassandra.config                             = /debezium/config/cassandra.yaml
cassandra.driver.config.file                 = /debezium/config/driver.conf
snapshot.mode                                = NEVER
snapshot.consistency                         = QUORUM
commit.log.relocation.dir                    = /debezium/data/cdc_relocation
commit.log.real.time.processing.enabled      = true
commit.log.marked.complete.poll.interval.ms  = 100
cdc.dir.poll.interval.ms                     = 100
poll.interval.ms                             = 100
offset.storage                               = org.apache.kafka.connect.storage.FileOffsetBackingStore
offset.storage.file.filename                 = /debezium/data/offsets.dat
http.port                                    = 8040
```

Sink configuration:

```
DEBEZIUM_SINK_TYPE                  = http
DEBEZIUM_SINK_HTTP_URL              = ...
DEBEZIUM_FORMAT_KEY                 = json
DEBEZIUM_FORMAT_VALUE               = json
DEBEZIUM_FORMAT_KEY_SCHEMAS_ENABLE  = false
DEBEZIUM_FORMAT_VALUE_SCHEMAS_ENABLE = false
```

`cassandra.yaml` is a copy of the Cassandra 4.1.4 node config with `GossipingPropertyFileSnitch` replaced by `SimpleSnitch`.

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

<!-- E.g. on-premises, with a specific cloud provider, etc. -->

On-premise Cassandra 4.1.4 with CDC enabled (`cdc_enabled: true`). 

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

The Cassandra 4 source task starts, attaches to the node, and streams CDC commit-log events to the HTTP sink.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

Debezium Server boots, loads the Cassandra 4 connector, validates the configuration, creates the source task, and then the task fails to start with a `NullPointerException`. The engine transitions `STARTING_TASKS -> STOPPING -> STOPPED`, the process reports the connector completed with `success = 'false'`, and the container exits with code 0.

The failure occurs in `AbstractConnectorTask.preStart` because `this.template` is null:

```
io.debezium.embedded.async.AsyncEmbeddedEngine - Task #1 (out of 1 tasks) failed to start. Failed with
java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException: java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke
  "io.debezium.connector.cassandra.CassandraConnectorTaskTemplate.getTaskContext()" because "this.template" is null
    at java.base/java.util.concurrent.FutureTask.report(FutureTask.java:122)
    at java.base/java.util.concurrent.FutureTask.get(FutureTask.java:191)
    at io.debezium.embedded.async.AsyncEmbeddedEngine.startSourceTasks(AsyncEmbeddedEngine.java:473)
    at io.debezium.embedded.async.AsyncEmbeddedEngine.run(AsyncEmbeddedEngine.java:220)
    at io.debezium.server.DebeziumServer.lambda$start$0(DebeziumServer.java:166)
    ...
Caused by: java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke
  "io.debezium.connector.cassandra.CassandraConnectorTaskTemplate.getTaskContext()" because "this.template" is null
    at io.debezium.connector.cassandra.AbstractConnectorTask.preStart(AbstractConnectorTask.java:52)
    at io.debezium.connector.common.BaseSourceTask.start(BaseSourceTask.java:253)
    at io.debezium.embedded.async.AsyncEmbeddedEngine.lambda$startSourceTasks$2(AsyncEmbeddedEngine.java:452)
    ...
```

During the subsequent stop, a second NPE is raised for the same null `template` field:

```
io.debezium.connector.cassandra.exceptions.CassandraConnectorTaskException:
  java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke
  "io.debezium.connector.cassandra.CassandraConnectorTaskTemplate.stopAll()" because "this.template" is null
    at io.debezium.connector.cassandra.AbstractConnectorTask.doStop(AbstractConnectorTask.java:106)
    at io.debezium.connector.common.BaseSourceTask.stop(BaseSourceTask.java:529)
    ...
Caused by: java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke
  "io.debezium.connector.cassandra.CassandraConnectorTaskTemplate.stopAll()" because "this.template" is null
    at io.debezium.connector.cassandra.AbstractConnectorTask.doStop(AbstractConnectorTask.java:103)
```

The last log lines before the source task threads run, `CassandraSourceInfoStructMaker` is loaded but the task never reaches connection to the Cassandra node:

```

  io.debezium.connector.cassandra.CassandraSourceInfoStructMaker
io.debezium.embedded.async.AsyncEmbeddedEngine - Task #1 (out of 1 tasks) failed to start.
```

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

<!-- Ideally, also verify with the latest Alpha/Beta/CR version. -->

Observed on `3.6.0.Alpha2` (the latest 3.6 build published on quay.io at time of report; no `3.6.0.Final` exists yet). The same `CassandraConnectorTaskTemplate.getTaskContext()` / `this.template` null NPE was previously observed on `3.4.0` and `3.4.1`.

Yes. The connector was run with DEBUG-level logging. Relevant excerpt (full boot to exit):

title: CockroachDB connector deduplicates events by unqualified table name, colliding across schemas
body:
The CockroachDB connector deduplicates incoming changefeed events using a key built from the changefeed message's `source.table_name` field, which is **unqualified** (the table name only, without its schema).

After multi-schema capture was added in debezium/dbz#1973, the connector can consume two tables that share a name in different schemas (for example `public.orders` and `inventory.orders`). If both change at the same MVCC timestamp with the same operation, they produce the **same** dedup key, and one event is silently dropped.

The connector already has the authoritative, schema-qualified `TableId` at the point of deduplication (it is derived from the Kafka topic the event arrived on). The dedup key should be built from that `TableId` instead of from the message body. This also removes the connector's only functional dependency on the `source` block being present in the enriched envelope.

**Fix**

- Build the deduplication key from the schema-qualified `TableId` (`identifier()`), plus the operation and the event timestamp, rather than from `source.table_name`.

**Tests**

- Unit test asserting that two same-named tables in different schemas produce distinct dedup keys, that the key is stable for the same table/op/timestamp, and that it does not depend on the `source` block being present.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDB connector deduplicates events by unqualified table name, colliding across schemas

title: CockroachDB connector should default enriched_properties to source and treat the value as a passthrough
body:
The `cockroachdb.changefeed.enriched.properties` property currently defaults to `source,schema` and its description lists `mvcc` as an accepted value. Two problems:

1. **`schema` is not used by the connector.** The connector derives the schema of each event from the table metadata it discovers over JDBC, and it identifies each event's table from the topic the event arrives on. It does not read the enriched `schema` block. Requesting `schema` by default adds a large, redundant block to every message that the connector discards. (This holds for schema-change handling too: evolution is detected by comparing event fields against the JDBC-discovered model, not from the message's `schema` block.)
2. **`mvcc` is not a valid `enriched_properties` value.** CockroachDB only accepts `source` and `schema`; `mvcc` is a separate top-level changefeed option, not an `enriched_properties` value.

The connector functionally requires only the base `enriched` envelope (`op`, `ts_ns`, `after`, and `before` when `diff` is set). The `source` block is still worth requesting by default because it carries the originating database, schema, table, and commit timestamp, which is useful for operators inspecting the intermediate topics and for other consumers of them.

**Proposed changes**

- Default `cockroachdb.changefeed.enriched.properties` to `source`.
- Treat the value as a passthrough to the CockroachDB `CREATE CHANGEFEED` statement, with no hardcoded allowlist of property names. CockroachDB owns the valid set (currently `source`, `schema`) and validates it at changefeed creation, so an allowlist in the connector would block adoption of a future CockroachDB value. This mirrors how the PostgreSQL connector treats `slot.stream.params`.
- Surface CockroachDB's error message directly when `CREATE CHANGEFEED` fails (invalid option, missing privilege, bad URI), so misconfiguration is visible in the task status rather than only in the cause chain.
- Update the property description and the connector documentation accordingly.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDB connector should default enriched_properties to source and treat the value as a passthrough

title: CockroachDB connector accepts envelope values it cannot parse (wrapped, bare)
body:
`cockroachdb.changefeed.envelope` validated against `enriched`, `wrapped`, and `bare`, but the connector's event parser only handles the `enriched` envelope. Setting either other value passed config validation and created the changefeed, then failed or silently misbehaved at runtime:

- **`bare`** emits row data at the top level with metadata under `__crdb__`, with no `after`/`before` wrapper, so the parser cannot extract change data at all.
- **`wrapped`** has `after`/`before` but no `op` and no `ts_ns`. Operation type degrades to before/after guessing, and because the deduplication key is `tableId:op:ts_ns`, the missing `ts_ns` collapses every same-operation change on a table to a single key, so events after the first are silently deduplicated away.

Only `enriched` carries the `op` and `ts_ns` fields the connector relies on.

**Fix**

Because `enriched` is the only valid value, the option could only ever be set wrong. Rather than keep the option and validate it back to a single value, remove it:

- Remove the `cockroachdb.changefeed.envelope` property. The connector always creates changefeeds with `envelope='enriched'` internally.
- Guard the existing-changefeed reuse path: if a running changefeed matches the connector's topic prefix and tables but was not created with `envelope='enriched'`, fail to start with a clear message instead of consuming a feed the connector cannot parse.
- Update the property documentation and examples.

Existing configurations that still set `cockroachdb.changefeed.envelope=enriched` continue to work: Kafka Connect ignores the now-unknown property with a warning.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDB connector accepts envelope values it cannot parse (wrapped, bare)

title: CockroachDBEndToEndIT passes without verifying end-to-end delivery
body:
`CockroachDBEndToEndIT.shouldStartTaskAndProduceSourceRecords` is meant to validate the full pipeline (CockroachDB changefeed → intermediate Kafka → connector consumer → Debezium SourceRecords), but it passes even when zero records flow: it hard-asserts only that the task starts, and if no records are received it logs a warning and returns successfully.

In CI it collects zero records, for two reasons:

1. The test manually pre-creates a changefeed with `topic_name=...`, but the connector's reuse detection matches on `topic_prefix=...`, so the connector does not recognize it and tries to create its own changefeed.
2. The connector is configured with `cockroachdb.changefeed.sink.uri = kafka://localhost:<host-mapped-port>`. CockroachDB runs in its own container, so `localhost` is the CockroachDB container, not the host. `CREATE CHANGEFEED` fails with `unable to dial ... connection refused`, the connector retries indefinitely, and no events are produced.

**Fix**

- Let the connector own the changefeed and point its sink URI at the internal Kafka network alias (`kafka://kafka:9092`), which CockroachDB can reach on the shared Testcontainers network.
- Point the connector's own consumer at the host-mapped Kafka port via `cockroachdb.changefeed.kafka.bootstrap.servers`, since the consumer runs in the test JVM.
- Insert data after the connector starts so the changefeed captures it.
- Require that create, update, and delete SourceRecords are received, instead of passing on zero.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDBEndToEndIT passes without verifying end-to-end delivery

title: Cache LOB values in ReselectColumnPostProcessor
body:
# Feature request or enhancement

## Which use case/requirement will be addressed by the proposed feature?

Add an **optional, pluggable cache** to the `ReselectColumnsPostProcessor` so that re-selected column
values can be reused across change events instead of issuing a database query for every event.

### Background

The reselect post-processor re-queries column values when an event carries the *unavailable value
placeholder* (e.g. unchanged PostgreSQL TOAST columns, Oracle LOBs) or a `null` the user wants
refreshed. Today **every** qualifying event triggers a `SELECT` against the source database.

For workloads with large out-of-line columns that change rarely but sit on frequently-updated rows,
this produces a high volume of repeated, identical re-selection queries for the same `(table, key,
column)` tuple. On managed databases (e.g. Aurora endpoints) this adds measurable load and latency to
the streaming pipeline, even though the re-selected value has not changed between events.

### Proposed behaviour

- Cache exposed behind a **strategy interface** (`ReselectColumnCache`) so alternative backends (e.g.
  an embedded key/value store such as RocksDB, or a user-custom implementation) can be plugged in
  without changing the post-processor. A default in-memory implementation is provided and selected via
  `reselect.cache.type`.
- The cache is organised **hierarchically** as `table -> row -> column`. The post-processor resolves a
  row-scoped handle once per event (`forRow(tableId, keyValues)`), so the table and row-key identity
  are serialized a single time and reused for every column of that row — important for tables with
  multiple LOB/TOAST columns.
- On a qualifying event, look up each required column in the cache; only the **misses** are queried,
  in a single `SELECT`. Hits are served from the cache.
- **Invalidate-on-modify**: whenever an event carries a *real* (non-placeholder) value for a column,
  its cache entry is evicted. This guarantees a later placeholder event for the same row never serves
  a value that has since changed — so cache correctness does **not** depend on the TTL.
- The default in-memory cache is bounded by size (LRU on the number of cached rows, via the existing
  `io.debezium.util.BoundedConcurrentHashMap`) and by a per-value TTL that only bounds memory retention
  for cold rows.

### Configuration (all optional, cache disabled by default)

| Property | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `reselect.cache.enabled` | `false` | Enable the re-selection cache. |
| `reselect.cache.type` | `io.debezium.processors.reselect.cache.MemoryReselectColumnCache` | The `ReselectColumnCache` implementation class. |
| `reselect.cache.max.size` | `10000` | Maximum number of cached rows (LRU eviction). Default in-memory cache only. |
| `reselect.cache.ttl.ms` | `600000` | Entry time-to-live in milliseconds. Default in-memory cache only. |

When `reselect.cache.enabled=false` (the default) the post-processor behaves exactly as before — no
behavioural change for existing users.

### Why this is safe

Re-selection normally exists to fetch the **latest** committed state, so naive caching would risk
emitting stale data. The invalidate-on-modify rule closes that gap: any event that actually changes a
column refreshes/evicts its cached value before the next placeholder event can read it. The TTL is
therefore a memory bound, not the correctness mechanism, which is why a relatively long default
(10 minutes) is acceptable.

### Scope / non-goals

- No new third-party dependency — the default cache reuses `io.debezium.util.BoundedConcurrentHashMap`
  already used across the connector-common module.
- No distributed/shared cache by default — the in-memory implementation is per-task and in-process.
  Shared/persistent backends can be added as separate `ReselectColumnCache` strategies.

---

## Implementation ideas (optional)

- Strategy interface `io.debezium.processors.reselect.cache.ReselectColumnCache` with a row-scoped
  `RowCache` handle, plus a default `MemoryReselectColumnCache`, in `debezium-connector-common`.
- The in-memory implementation uses a hierarchical `row -> (column -> value)` structure so the row
  identity is serialized once per event and shared across all of that row's columns.
- Cached values are the **raw** JDBC values; the existing `getConvertedValue(...)` conversion is
  applied uniformly to both cache hits and freshly queried values, so caching does not interfere with
  value conversion.
- `byte[]` key values are rendered by content (not identity) so binary primary keys produce stable,
  collision-free cache keys.
- Happy to adjust naming, defaults, or split the change as the maintainers prefer.
author: gaurav7261

project_fields.Title: Cache LOB values in ReselectColumnPostProcessor

title: Implement Week 1: Project skeleton, event models, and ingestion handlers for PyDebeziumAI
body:
This issue tracks the Week 1 scope for the PyDebeziumAI GSoC 2026 project. 

The goal is to implement the core ingestion and data representation foundation of the Python library:

1. **Ingestion handlers**:
   - `ConnectIngestionHandler`: Reads raw Kafka Connect `SourceRecord` objects directly from the memory-bridge JVM context (using JPype).
   - `JsonIngestionHandler`: Consumes Debezium events serialized as standard JSON strings.
2. **Canonical event models**:
   - Normalizes different CDC structures into validated Pydantic v2 models (`DebeziumEventModel` and `DebeziumPayloadModel`).
3. **Logical Type Conversion**:
   - Decodes Debezium's custom formats (VariableScaleDecimals, decimals, zoned times, micro/nano durations, and geometries) into native Python types.
4. **Validation**:
   - Initial unit tests validating Pydantic model serialization and primary key extraction.
author: KMohnishM

project_fields.Title: Implement Week 1: Project skeleton, event models, and ingestion handlers for PyDebeziumAI

title: Transactional pg_logical_emit_message() events are emitted twice after connector restart
body:
**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

main(HEAD)

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

```
% cat docker-min-no-table.json
{
  "name": "test-connector",
  "config": {
    "connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.postgresql.PostgresConnector",
    "database.hostname": "postgres",
    "database.port": "5432",
    "database.user": "postgres",
    "database.password": "postgres",
    "database.dbname": "postgres",
    "topic.prefix": "example",
    "slot.name": "emit_log_message",
    "plugin.name": "pgoutput",
    "publication.autocreate.mode": "no_tables",
    "publication.name": "notable",
    "snapshot.mode": "no_data"
  }
}
```


---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

_E.g. on-premises, with a specific cloud provider, etc._

on-premise

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

When a message is emitted using `pg_logical_emit_message()` with `transactional = true` before the connector is stopped, the message should be delivered exactly once after the connector is restarted.

According to the Debezium documentation, the PostgreSQL source connector supports Exactly Once Delivery:

https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/configuration/eos.html

---

**What behavior do you see?**

After restarting the connector, the same transactional logical message is delivered twice.

For example, a message emitted via:

```
=# BEGIN;
=# SELECT * FROM pg_logical_emit_message(true, 'test1', 'aaa');
=# COMMIT;
```

is published twice to the Kafka topic after connector restart.

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

Yes.

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

_You might be asked later to provide [DEBUG/TRACE](https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/operations/logging.html) level log._

Yes. I can provide them if needed.

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**

Start the PostgreSQL connector using the configuration above:

```
$ curl -i -X POST -H "Accept:application/json" -H  "Content-Type:application/json" http://localhost:8083/connectors/ -d @docker-min-no-table.json
```

Emit a transactional logical message:

```
=# BEGIN; SELECT * FROM pg_logical_emit_message(true, 'test1', 'aaa'); COMMIT;
```

Stop the connector.

Restart the connector.

Consume from the message topic:

```
$ docker-compose -f docker-compose-postgres.yaml exec kafka /kafka/bin/kafka-console-consumer.sh \
    --bootstrap-server kafka:9092 \
    --from-beginning \
    --property print.key=true \
    --topic example.message

```

Observe that two identical records with prefix test1 are present in the topic, although only one message was emitted:


title: Oracle STORE-syntax DDL fails to parse due to quoted segment name
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

When using any one of the following SQL staetments, Oracle DDL parser fails to parse:
```
CREATE TABLE T (X CLOB)
    LOB ("X") STORE AS BASICFILE "X_SEG"
        (ENABLE STORAGE IN ROW CHUNK 8192 RETENTION NOCACHE LOGGING);

CREATE TABLE T (X CLOB)
    LOB ("X") STORE AS SECUREFILE "X_SEG"
        (ENABLE STORAGE IN ROW DEDUPLICATE COMPRESS MEDIUM NOCACHE LOGGING);

ALTER TABLE T MOVE LOB ("X") STORE AS SECUREFILE "X_SEG"
    (ENABLE STORAGE IN ROW DEDUPLICATE COMPRESS MEDIUM NOCACHE LOGGING);

ALTER TABLE T MOVE LOB ("X") STORE AS BASICFILE "X_SEG"
    (ENABLE STORAGE IN ROW CHUNK 8192 RETENTION NOCACHE LOGGING);

ALTER TABLE T MOVE ONLINE LOB ("X") STORE AS SECUREFILE "X_SEG"
    (ENABLE STORAGE IN ROW DEDUPLICATE COMPRESS MEDIUM NOCACHE LOGGING);

ALTER TABLE T MOVE ONLINE LOB ("X") STORE AS BASICFILE "X_SEG"
    (ENABLE STORAGE IN ROW CHUNK 8192 RETENTION NOCACHE LOGGING);
```

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

_E.g. on-premises, with a specific cloud provider, etc._

Oracle 19c

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

_Ideally, also verify with the latest Alpha/Beta/CR version._

Yes on `main`.

title: Support for engine filtering and multi connector engine
body:
## Context

  The engine's startup path previously obtained a single `DebeziumConnectorRegistry` from the CDI container and iterated its engines directly. This worked for single-connector deployments but had no way to aggregate engines from multiple connectors (e.g., Postgres + MySQL running side by side), and no mechanism to selectively start only a subset of those engines.

## Changes

We should support multi connector engines with filtering rule for startup

title: BaseSourceTask ignores RetriableException count of errors.max.retries
body:
## Bug report

When a source connector enters RESTARTING after a retriable error, BaseSourceTask tries to restart the connector using start(). If start() then throws a RetriableException, like if the source DB is still unavailable, the exception propagates unhandled, and errors.max.retries has no effect on the number of restart attempts.

Proposed fix in the PR here: https://github.com/debezium/debezium/pull/7497

---

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

This is a debezium-core bug affecting all connectors. Reproduce with any connector (e.g., Debezium Connector for PostgreSQL or MySQL). Initially found while using debezium-connector-oracle. Version: 3.4.0.Final

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

The key variable pertinent to this bug is errors.max.retries. When set to a positive number, the number of retries is not respected.

`migrator:
  connector:
    source:
      common:
        name: "debezium-source-connector-${jobId}"

        database.user: "${databaseUser}"
        database.password: "${databasePassword}"
        database.port: "${databasePort}"

        snapshot.mode: "${snapshotMode}"
        snapshot.select.statement.overrides: "${overriddenTableNames}"

        table.include.list: "${whitelistedTableNames}"
        include.schema.changes: false

        event.processing.failure.handling.mode: "${errorHandlingMode}"

        schema.history.internal.store.only.captured.tables.ddl: true
        schema.history.internal.kafka.bootstrap.servers: "${migrator.kafka.internal.bootstrap.servers:${migrator.kafka.bootstrap.servers}}"
        schema.history.internal.kafka.topic: "schema.history-${jobId}"

        errors.max.retries: 3
        errors.retry.delay.initial.ms: 30000
        errors.retry.delay.max.ms: 60000
        retriable.restart.connector.wait.ms: 60000

        "[database.${jdbcPropertyKey}]": "${jdbcPropertyValue}"

        tombstones.on.delete: false
        tasks.max: 1
        key.converter: org.apache.kafka.connect.json.JsonConverter
        value.converter: org.apache.kafka.connect.json.JsonConverter
        key.converter.schemas.enable: true
        value.converter.schemas.enable: true

        offset.storage: "org.apache.kafka.connect.storage.KafkaOffsetBackingStore"
        offset.storage.topic: "offset.topic-${jobId}"
        offset.storage.partitions: 1

        topic.prefix: "${databaseName}"
        topic.creation.default.replication.factor: 1
        topic.creation.default.partitions: 1

      ORACLE:
        database.url: "${databaseUrl}"
        database.dbname: "${databaseName}"
        database.pdb.name: "${oraclePdbName}"
        connector.class: "io.debezium.connector.oracle.OracleConnector"
        lob.enabled: true

    sink:
      common:
        name: "migrator-sink-connector-${jobId}"

        connector.class:[...]

        tasks.max: 1

        topics: "${topics}"
        context.transform: "${serializedTransformContext}"
        context.load: "${serializedLoadContext}"
        job.id: "${jobId}"
        partitions.max: "${migrator.connector.source.common.topic.creation.default.partitions}"
        errors.max.retries: 21 
        errors.retry.delay.initial.ms: 30000
        errors.retry.delay.max.ms: 300000

        key.converter: ${migrator.connector.source.common.key.converter}
        value.converter: ${migrator.connector.source.common.value.converter}
        key.converter.schemas.enable: ${migrator.connector.source.common.key.converter.schemas.enable}
        value.converter.schemas.enable:  ${migrator.connector.source.common.value.converter.schemas.enable}

        unavailable.value.placeholder: "__debezium_unavailable_value"

  kafka:
    enable:
      topic:
        cleanup: true
`

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

_E.g. on-premises, with a specific cloud provider, etc._

Tested with AWS hosted Oracle DB, but reproducible with other DB providers and hosting options.

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

When the source connector is in `RESTARTING` and reconnect fails with `RetriableException`, the failure should count towards `error.max.retries`. Once the limit is reached, it should fail and terminate with a `ConnectException`.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

If the source DB remains unavailable during restart attempts, `RetriableException` from `start()` propagates out of `poll()` unhandled. `errors.max.retries` is ignored.

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

_Ideally, also verify with the latest Alpha/Beta/CR version._

Yes (3.4.0.Final).

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

_You might be asked later to provide [DEBUG/TRACE](https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/operations/logging.html) level log._

`<Your answer>`

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**
1. Start a connector against a database.
2. Set errors.max.retries=2 and retriable.restart.wait.ms=5000.
3. Stop the database after the connector is running to trigger a RetriableException and put the connector into RESTARTING state.
4. Keep the database stopped.
5. Observe that the connector retries indefinitely and never fails, even after exceeding errors.max.retries.
author: DanGrabski

project_fields.Title: BaseSourceTask ignores RetriableException count of errors.max.retries

title: QuantileMeter spawns unnecessary background threads for percentile computation
body:
## Summary

The Spanner connector creates 11 `QuantileMeter` instances (one per `Statistics` object), each spawning a dedicated background thread that polls a `LinkedBlockingQueue` in a tight loop to feed values into a DDSketch for p50/p95/p99 quantile computation. Under sustained high-throughput workloads, these 11 threads collectively consume ~50% of a single core.

## Root Cause

`QuantileMeter` uses a producer-consumer pattern with a `BlockingQueue` and a dedicated `Thread` to feed values into a `DDSketch`. This was likely done to avoid synchronization on the hot path, but `DDSketch.accept()` is O(1) and extremely fast - the thread+queue indirection adds more overhead than it saves.

```java
private final BlockingQueue<Double> queue = new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(1000);
private final Thread thread;
// Thread polls queue in a loop, calls sketch.accept(value)
```

## Impact

- 11 threads consuming ~50% of one CPU core under load
- Thread stack memory overhead (~11 MB for 11 threads)
- Bounded queue (size 1000) can silently drop values under burst load, reducing quantile accuracy

## Proposed Fix

- Remove the `Thread`, `BlockingQueue`, and poll loop from `QuantileMeter`
- Make `addValue()` call `synchronized sketch.accept(value)` directly
- Handle the `clearInterval` timeout check inline in `addValue()`
- `start()` initializes the `TimeoutMeter` instead of starting a thread
- `shutdown()` calls `reset()` instead of interrupting a thread
- All public method signatures and quantile metrics remain unchanged

## Performance Test Report

We ran performance tests on a 1-node Spanner instance with 1 CKU Dedicated cluster, 1 task, 2-hour test window in Confluent Cloud. Both builds include the CommittedRecord memory leak fix (debezium/dbz#1992).

### Without QuantileMeter fix (11 background threads present)

| Format | msg/s | Peak JVM Heap | Peak JVM CPU | Peak Pod CPU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AVRO | 33,276 | 5.08 GB | 91.9% | 3.09 cores |
| JSON_SR | 43,103 | 7.89 GB | 81.1% | 3.22 cores |
| PROTOBUF | 34,480 | 6.95 GB | 82.8% | 3.27 cores |

### With QuantileMeter fix (threads eliminated, synchronous DDSketch)

| Format | msg/s | Peak JVM Heap | Peak JVM CPU | Peak Pod CPU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AVRO | 55,335 | 3.9 GB | 68.3% | 2.77 cores |
| JSON_SR | 42,078 | 7.0 GB | 67.9% | 2.71 cores |
| PROTOBUF | 54,721 | 4.5 GB | 69.1% | 2.80 cores |

### Summary

| Metric | Without fix | With fix | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| AVRO msg/s | 33,276 | **55,335** | **+66%** |
| PROTOBUF msg/s | 34,480 | **54,721** | **+59%** |
| Peak JVM CPU % | 81-92% | 68-69% | **-26%** |
| Peak Pod CPU (cores) | 3.09-3.27 | 2.71-2.80 | **-14%** |
| Peak JVM Heap (AVRO) | 5.08 GB | 3.9 GB | **-23%** |

Eliminating 11 `QuantileMeter` background threads and making `DDSketch.accept()` synchronous inline reduced CPU usage by ~26%, pod CPU by ~14%, and improved throughput by 59-66% for AVRO and PROTOBUF. The bounded `LinkedBlockingQueue(1000)` that could silently drop values under burst is also removed, improving quantile accuracy.
author: sauravkumarrr

project_fields.Title: QuantileMeter spawns unnecessary background threads for percentile computation

title: Sqlserver connector: Data loss with direct mode on update with  fetch size > 0
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

SQL Server connector. Reproduced on the **latest `main` (3.6.0-SNAPSHOT)**, built and run locally. The behavior also affects earlier releases (the relevant code is unchanged).

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

Key settings that trigger it:
```json
{
  "connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.sqlserver.SqlServerConnector",
  "data.query.mode": "direct",
  "streaming.fetch.size": "5",
  "snapshot.mode": "no_data",
  "database.names": "bgoyal_test",
  "table.include.list": "dbo.users"
}
```
`streaming.fetch.size` is set to `5` here to reproduce minimally; in production it is the default `10000` (any non-zero value reproduces it once the transaction exceeds the fetch boundary).

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

Microsoft SQL Server 2019 (RTM-CU32) 15.0.4430.1, self-managed (VM/EC2). CDC enabled via `sys.sp_cdc_enable_table`.

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

Every change captured by SQL Server CDC for a committed transaction should be emitted to Kafka exactly once, independent of `data.query.mode` and `streaming.fetch.size`. An update to a unique-index column should produce all of its delete/insert events.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

Data loss of update events when using `data.query.mode=direct` with `streaming.fetch.size` > 0. The loss happens only when the update is to a column that is part of the table's **unique index** — SQL Server records this as a "deferred update", i.e. a **delete followed by an insert with the same `__$seqval`**. If the transaction is a large bulk update that does not fully return in one fetch (because of `streaming.fetch.size`), the attempt to fetch the rest of the transaction returns only a subset of the rows, so some events are silently dropped.

Example. Table:
```sql
CREATE TABLE dbo.users (
    id         INT          NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY CLUSTERED,
    email      VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    name       VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    updated_at DATETIME     NOT NULL DEFAULT getutcdate()
);
CREATE UNIQUE NONCLUSTERED INDEX UX_users_email ON dbo.users(email);
EXEC sys.sp_cdc_enable_table @source_schema='dbo', @source_name='users',
     @role_name=NULL, @supports_net_changes=0;
```
Update to the unique-index column:
```sql
UPDATE dbo.users SET email = name + '_v0@example.com' WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 5;
```
On the CDC table this produces 10 rows (5 deletes + 5 inserts). Ordered as the **direct-mode** query orders them (`ORDER BY __$start_lsn, __$command_id, __$seqval, __$operation`):
```
commit_lsn            |seqval                |__$command_id|__$operation|name |email
----------------------+----------------------+-------------+------------+-----+--------------------
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80003|            1|           1|alice|alice_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80004|            2|           1|bob  |bob_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80005|            3|           1|carol|carol_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80006|            4|           1|dave |dave_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80007|            5|           1|eve  |eve_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80003|            6|           2|alice|alice_v0@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80004|            7|           2|bob  |bob_v0@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80005|            8|           2|carol|carol_v0@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80006|            9|           2|dave |dave_v0@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80007|           10|           2|eve  |eve_v0@example.com
```
Ordered as the **function-mode** query orders them (`ORDER BY __$start_lsn, __$seqval, __$operation` — no `__$command_id`), the delete/insert of each row stay adjacent and `__$seqval` is monotonic:
```
commit_lsn            |seqval                |__$operation|name |email
----------------------+----------------------+------------+-----+--------------------
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80003|           1|alice|alice_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80003|           2|alice|alice_v0@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80004|           1|bob  |bob_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80004|           2|bob  |bob_v0@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80005|           1|carol|carol_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80005|           2|carol|carol_v0@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80006|           1|dave |dave_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80006|           2|dave |dave_v0@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80007|           1|eve  |eve_v1@example.com
0x00000a24000018a80015|0x00000a24000018a80007|           2|eve  |eve_v0@example.com
```
With `streaming.fetch.size=5`, the Kafka topic receives **5 deletes and only 1 create** (the `eve` / `command_id 10` row). The 4 inserts for `alice/bob/carol/dave` are missing:
```
op=d id=1 change_lsn=00000a24:000018a8:0003
op=d id=2 change_lsn=00000a24:000018a8:0004
op=d id=3 change_lsn=00000a24:000018a8:0005
op=d id=4 change_lsn=00000a24:000018a8:0006
op=d id=5 change_lsn=00000a24:000018a8:0007
op=c id=5 change_lsn=00000a24:000018a8:0007 event_serial_no=2     <-- only surviving insert
```
All inserts with `seqval < 00000a24:000018a8:0007` are dropped. Only `command_id 10` survives because its `seqval` (`...0007`) is `>=` the last-seen `seqval` and its operation is `> 1`. The issue reproduces whenever the number of (deferred) updates exceeds the `streaming.fetch.size` boundary.

**Likely cause:** the first fetch returns the 5 deletes (`command_id 1–5`, ordered by `command_id`), so the resume position becomes the *highest delete* `seqval` (`...0007`). The next fetch resumes with `getNextResultSet()` using a `WHERE __$seqval > ?` predicate that does **not** account for `__$command_id`, so the inserts whose `seqval` is below that high-water mark are skipped. In other words, in direct mode the query `ORDER BY` uses `__$command_id` but the pagination resume key uses only `__$seqval`, and the two disagree.

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

Yes — reproduced on the latest `main` (3.6.0-SNAPSHOT) built locally. It does **not** occur with `data.query.mode=function` (whose `ORDER BY` has no `__$command_id`, so `__$seqval` stays monotonic and delete/insert pairs remain adjacent).

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

```
TRACE  Getting top 5 changes for table Capture instance "dbo_orders" [...changeTableId=...cdc.dbo_orders_CT...] in range [00000a23:0001b318:0010-00000000:00000000:0000-0, 00000a23:0001b838:0015]   (io.debezium...SqlServerConnection:421)
TRACE  Processing change ChangeTableResultSet{... currentChangePosition=00000a23:0001b838:0015(00000a23:0001b838:0003,1)}   (...SqlServerStreamingChangeEventSource:314)
TRACE  Processing change ChangeTableResultSet{... currentChangePosition=00000a23:0001b838:0015(00000a23:0001b838:0008,1)}
TRACE  Processing change ChangeTableResultSet{... currentChangePosition=00000a23:0001b838:0015(00000a23:0001b838:000b,1)}
TRACE  Processing change ChangeTableResultSet{... currentChangePosition=00000a23:0001b838:0015(00000a23:0001b838:000e,1)}
TRACE  Processing change ChangeTableResultSet{... currentChangePosition=00000a23:0001b838:0015(00000a23:0001b838:0011,1)}
TRACE  Getting top 5 changes for table Capture instance "dbo_orders" [...] in range [00000a23:0001b838:0015-00000a23:0001b838:0011-1, 00000a23:0001b838:0015]   (io.debezium...SqlServerConnection:421)
INFO   11 records sent during previous ... last recorded offset ... is {event_serial_no=1, commit_lsn=00000a23:0001b838:0015, change_lsn=00000a23:0001b838:0011}   (io.debezium...BaseSourceTask:387)
TRACE  Processing change ChangeTableResultSet{... currentChangePosition=00000a23:0001b838:0015(00000a23:0001b838:0011,2)}   <-- only the op2 insert at the high-water seqval survives
```

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our tutorial deployment?**

1. Create the `dbo.users` table and unique nonclustered index, enable CDC (DDL above).
2. Insert 5 rows; start the SQL Server connector with `data.query.mode=direct`, `streaming.fetch.size=5`, `snapshot.mode=no_data`, capturing `dbo.users`.
3. In a single transaction, update the unique-index column on all 5 rows:
   `UPDATE dbo.users SET email = name + '_v0@example.com' WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 5;`
4. Consume the topic: expected 5 deletes + 5 creates; actual 5 deletes + 1 create (4 rows lost). Increase the row count / `fetch.size` to confirm it scales with the fetch boundary.

The potential cause seems to be the `getNextResultSet()` call not taking `__$command_id` into account in direct mode.
author: b-goyal

project_fields.Title: Sqlserver connector: Data loss with direct mode on update with  fetch size > 0

title: CockroachDB connector forces a dot separator on the topic prefix and ignores topic_name in the sink URI
body:
Two related topic-naming problems, reported by a user whose Kafka topics are governed by ACLs:

1. **Forced dot separator.** The connector appends a literal `.` to the configured topic prefix when building the changefeed (`topic_prefix=<prefix>.`). A user whose prefix already ends with a separator (for example `gmf-c2c-prf1-prpd-`) gets a doubled separator in the topic name (`gmf-c2c-prf1-prpd-.<db>.<schema>.<table>`), which fails their topic authorization. The connector gives no way to control the separator.

2. **`topic_name`/`topic_prefix` in the sink URI is silently ignored.** If a user sets `topic_name=` (or `topic_prefix=`) directly in `cockroachdb.changefeed.sink.uri`, the connector still injects its own `topic_prefix` and computes consumer subscriptions from its own naming, so CockroachDB and the connector disagree on the topic and zero events flow, with no error.

**Fix**

- Treat `cockroachdb.changefeed.sink.topic.prefix` as a literal prefix: use it verbatim (no appended `.`) so the resulting topic is `<prefix><database>.<schema>.<table>` and the user controls the separator. When the prefix is not set, default to `<topic.prefix>.` to preserve the existing default topic layout. The consumer's topic computation is updated to match.
- Reject `topic_name`/`topic_prefix` in the sink URI at config-validation time with a clear message directing users to `cockroachdb.changefeed.sink.topic.prefix`.

The connector does not honor CockroachDB's `topic_name` option, because it routes per table using `topic_prefix` + `full_table_name`, whereas `topic_name` forces all tables onto one topic. The literal-prefix change already lets users reproduce any prefix-based naming they need.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDB connector forces a dot separator on the topic prefix and ignores topic_name in the sink URI

title: Allow splitting CockroachDB changefeeds across many tables to avoid performance coupling
body:
The connector always creates a single changefeed for all configured tables. That keeps it to one changefeed job (good for CockroachDB's recommended per-cluster changefeed limit), but CockroachDB advises against watching very many tables with a single changefeed because their performance becomes coupled: a slow table degrades the whole feed. Users capturing a large number of tables currently have no in-connector way to mitigate this.

**Enhancement**

- Add `cockroachdb.changefeed.max.tables.per.changefeed` (default `0` = all tables in one changefeed, current behavior). When set to a positive value, the connector splits the captured tables into multiple changefeeds of at most that many tables each, balancing coupling against the number of changefeed jobs. Per-group reuse detection is preserved so restarts stay idempotent. The consumer side is unchanged (it already subscribes to all per-table topics).
- Log a recommendation when a single changefeed would cover a large number of tables and splitting is not configured, so the coupling risk is surfaced rather than silent.
- Document the tradeoff (fewer feeds vs. coupling) and link to CockroachDB's changefeed best practices for the current recommended limits, rather than hardcoding a specific number.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: Allow splitting CockroachDB changefeeds across many tables to avoid performance coupling

title: Refactor YashanDB connector to clean up and align unused SourceInfo fields and event examples
body:
### Background
During the review of the YashanDB connector implementation, it was brought to light that there is significant overlap with the Oracle connector's SourceInfo setup. Many of these inherited fields and constants are never populated or used by YashanDB.

To ensure the connector is clean, maintainable, and contains only relevant data, we need to remove this dead code and update the corresponding documentation.

### Proposed Changes

SourceInfo.java: Remove unused fields (commitScn, eventScn, lcrPosition, userName, redoThread, rsld, ssn) along with their getter/setter methods. Remove unused constants: EVENT_SCN_KEY, COMMIT_SCN_KEY, LCR_POSITION_KEY, SNAPSHOT_KEY.

CommitScn.java: Delete this class. YashanDB YStream provides commit SCN directly via Position.getCommitScn(), making this multi-redo-thread tracking class unnecessary.

YashanDbOffsetContext.java:

Remove isCreateServer field and YSTREAM_SERVER_CREATE constant (dead round-trip flag stored to/loaded from offset but never read by business logic).

Remove CommitScn parameter from constructor, getCommitScn(), getOffset() store, and toString() reference.

YashanDbSourceInfoStructMaker.java: Remove COMMIT_SCN_KEY, REDO_THREAD_KEY, ROLLBACK_SEGMENT_ID_KEY, SQL_SEQUENCE_NUMBER_KEY schema fields and the commitScn.store() call.

YashanDbSnapshotChangeEventSource.java / YStreamOffsetContextLoader.java: Remove isCreateServer local variable and CommitScn.load() from offset loading.
author: Tlinian

project_fields.Title: Refactor YashanDB connector to clean up and align unused SourceInfo fields and event examples

title: Implement Week 2 transformation layer (document builder, ID strategy, and projection policy)
body:
### Description
Implement the **Transformation Layer** for `pydebeziumai` (GSoC Week 2 milestone). This layer is responsible for converting normalized database CDC row events (`DebeziumEventModel`) into semantic LangChain `Document` objects that are compatible with downstream vector stores.

### Sub-Tasks
- [ ] **Identity Strategy (`IdStrategy`)**: Implement ID generation strategies, including `TablePkIdStrategy` (generating stable vector IDs like `<table>:<primary_key>` to avoid document duplication on updates) and `CompositeIdStrategy`.
- [ ] **Projection Policy (`ProjectionPolicy`)**: Implement mechanisms to select which database fields are projected into the document search text (`page_content`) versus filterable attributes (`metadata`), supporting custom string formatting templates.
- [ ] **Document Builder (`DocumentBuilder`)**: Implement the core builder class that sanitizes logical database data types (Decimals to floats, datetimes to ISO strings, bytes to hex) and constructs standard LangChain `Document` objects.
- [ ] **Unit Tests**: Add comprehensive unit testing suite covering:
  - Template rendering correctness.
  - Composite key hashing stability.
  - Metadata type coercion safety.
author: KMohnishM

project_fields.Title: Implement Week 2 transformation layer (document builder, ID strategy, and projection policy)

title: Build Week 1: Platform Client Foundation and Pipeline Management Commands
body:
## Overview

As the pipeline subsystem has been prioritized over the build subsystem, Week 1 focuses on implementing the Platform Client foundation and all pipeline management commands for the Debezium JBang CLI.

## Goals

1. **Platform HTTP Client** - Java `HttpClient` wrapper for the Debezium 
   Platform REST API with support for:
   - `--api-url` flag for one-off commands
   - `~/.dbz/config.yaml` for persistent platform URL configuration

2. **`debezium pipeline list`** - `GET /pipelines`

3. **`debezium pipeline get <id>`** - `GET /pipelines/{id}`

4. **`debezium pipeline create --file pipeline.yaml`** - `POST /pipelines`

5. **`debezium pipeline update <id> --file pipeline.yaml`** - `PUT /pipelines/{id}`

6. **`debezium pipeline delete <id>`** - `DELETE /pipelines/{id}`

7. **Integration tests** for all pipeline commands

## Deliverable

A PR or sub-PRs to `debezium/jbang-catalog` with all pipeline CRUD commands 
implemented and tested.

title: Contradictory statements in the Debezium MongoDB Documentation
body:
## Bug report

In the change streams section of the documentation 
https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/connectors/mongodb.html#change-streams

"The MongoDB connector is also quite tolerant of changes in membership and leadership of the replica sets, of additions or removals of shards within a sharded cluster, and network problems that might cause communication failures. The connector always uses the replica set’s primary node to stream changes, so when the replica set undergoes ...."

So one would think that Debezium is streaming changes from the primary node of the replica set.

But just below that statement, the docs mention, in https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/connectors/mongodb.html#read-preference

"You specify read preferences for a MongoDB connection by setting the readPreference parameter in the [mongodb.connection.string](https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/connectors/mongodb.html#mongodb-property-mongodb-connection-string)."

**Does Debezium MongoDB open a change stream always via the primary, or does it honor the read preference?**

This same confusing documentation pattern has been present since at least version 2.7, https://debezium.io/documentation//reference/2.7/connectors/mongodb.html#read-preference
author: dutta1kartik3

title: CockroachDB connector's changefeed consumer cannot connect to a secured (TLS/mTLS/SASL) Kafka broker
body:
The connector has two Kafka interactions: CockroachDB pushing the changefeed to a Kafka sink (which supports mTLS via debezium/dbz#1974), and the connector's own `KafkaConsumer` reading those intermediate topics back to produce the Debezium events. The consumer only sets `bootstrap.servers`, `group.id`, deserializers, and `auto.offset.reset`, with no `security.protocol` and no SSL/SASL configuration, so it always connects in plaintext.

Against a secured broker (for example an mTLS listener on port 9093) the consumer is dropped during the handshake, with repeating `Cancelled in-flight API_VERSIONS request ... node disconnected` and `Bootstrap broker ... disconnected` messages, and no events flow. There is currently no way to give the consumer TLS or SASL settings, so the connector cannot be used end to end against a fully secured Kafka cluster.

**Fix**

- When `cockroachdb.changefeed.sink.tls.*` is set (the same PEM material used to push the changefeed over mTLS), derive the consumer's SSL configuration automatically: `security.protocol=SSL`, a PEM truststore from the CA, and, when a client certificate and key are present, a PEM keystore. This makes the mTLS round-trip work with no extra configuration.
- Add a `cockroachdb.changefeed.kafka.consumer.override.*` passthrough whose properties are applied verbatim to the consumer, following the convention Debezium uses for its schema-history and signal Kafka clients. This covers SASL, JKS stores, a differently-secured broker, and overriding any auto-derived value.

Adds unit tests for the auto-derive and passthrough, and updates the crdb-to-crdb-mtls example so the connector consumes over the mTLS listener (rather than a plaintext workaround).
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDB connector's changefeed consumer cannot connect to a secured (TLS/mTLS/SASL) Kafka broker

title: Explore sinkless (core) CockroachDB changefeeds to remove the intermediate Kafka hop
body:
The CockroachDB connector currently uses CockroachDB's Kafka-sink changefeed: CockroachDB pushes change events to an intermediate Kafka cluster, and the connector runs its own KafkaConsumer to read them back and re-produce them as standard Debezium events. This two-stage Kafka architecture is unique among Debezium source connectors (Postgres, MySQL, and others read the database's native stream directly and produce straight to the output topics through the Kafka Connect framework).

The intermediate hop is the source of several connector-specific concerns: configuring the connector's own consumer for TLS/SASL (debezium/dbz#2023), topic naming on the intermediate cluster (debezium/dbz#2013), changefeed-to-table grouping (debezium/dbz#2014), and the general operational overhead of a second Kafka client.

CockroachDB also supports sinkless (core) changefeeds via `EXPERIMENTAL CHANGEFEED FOR ...` (no `INTO`), which stream change events back over the SQL connection. If the connector consumed a sinkless changefeed over its existing JDBC/pgwire connection, it could produce directly to the output topics like a native Debezium connector, eliminating the intermediate Kafka cluster, the second Kafka client, and the entire class of consumer-side configuration. It would also inherit the worker's output Kafka configuration automatically.

This is a significant rearchitecture with trade-offs (the Kafka-sink model provides buffering and decoupling, and the enriched envelope availability and resume/offset semantics differ between sink and sinkless changefeeds), so it needs a proper design evaluation. Filing this to track the direction and weigh it against continuing to invest in the Kafka-sink model (additional sink types, consumer configuration, and so on).
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: Explore sinkless (core) CockroachDB changefeeds to remove the intermediate Kafka hop

title: Platform watcher may lead to unbounded WAL
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

DMP 3.4.0.Final

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

There should be no unbounded WAL growth.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

The default `debezium` replication slot is growing unbounded in a QA/TST environment.

See [#community-platform > Debezium replication slot size issue](https://debezium.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/392459-community-platform/topic/Debezium.20replication.20slot.20size.20issue/with/600105531)

Looking at the `ConductorEnvironmentWatcher`, if the watcher is enabled, we create a filtered replication slot and publication for the outbox table, but there is no `heartbeat.action.query` setup. Is this leading to unbounded WAL growth when other activity happens to other tables, but not the outbox? Should we enforce a heartbeat action query?

cc @mfvitale
author: Naros

title: OracleConnectorFilterIT - align LogMiner & XStream behavior
body:
In the test class, the method `shouldApplySchemaAndTableInclusionConfiguration` behaves differently when the adapter is XStream versus LogMiner, not because of differences in the connector, but simply because of how the container is set up. 

Specifically, XStream limits capture to the `DEBEZIUM` schema only, rather than to all schemas. This forces this method to make some assumptions in the test simply due to the setup. If the XStream adapter is set up differently, it would fail.

For simplicity in testing other deployment modes, like downstream mining, it would be easier to remove this difference, as applying filters on the XStream deployment during downstream mining did not work reliably.
author: Naros

project_fields.Title: OracleConnectorFilterIT - align LogMiner & XStream behavior

title: Add retry/back-off to XStream attach retry loop
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

_For feature requests or enhancements, please provide the following information:_

**Which use case/requirement will be addressed by the proposed feature?**

In the XStream adapter, if the connector fails to attach to the Outbound Server, it will attempt to reattach up to 10 times. Unfortunately, the reattach does not apply any retry-backoff and occurs in a short period of time. If during this ~10-20 seconds the previous process hasn't fully detached, the test will fail.

---

**Implementation ideas (optional)**

Apply an exponential DelayStrategy between each retry to allow for more sufficient time to attach safely.
author: Naros

project_fields.Title: Add retry/back-off to XStream attach retry loop

title: KafkaSchemaHistory recovery race (subscribe vs assign)
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

Debezium SQL Server connector, `3.5.2.Final`. The issue is in the shared `debezium-storage-kafka` module (`KafkaSchemaHistory`), so it is not specific to SQL Server - it affects every connector that uses Kafka-backed schema history.

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

```
{
    "connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.sqlserver.SqlServerConnector",
    "tasks.max": "1",
    "database.hostname": "sqlserver",
    "database.port": "1433",
    "database.user": "sa",
    "database.names": "testDB",
    "database.encrypt": "false",
    "topic.prefix": "server1",

    "schema.history.internal.kafka.bootstrap.servers": "broker:9092",
    "schema.history.internal.kafka.topic": "schema-changes.testdb",
    
    "schema.history.internal.kafka.recovery.attempts": "20",
    "schema.history.internal.kafka.recovery.poll.interval.ms": "100",
    "snapshot.mode": "initial"
  }
```
---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

_E.g. on-premises, with a specific cloud provider, etc._

SQL Server 2019 (`mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server:2019-latest`), single instance. Kafka Connect in distributed mode with multiple workers (the multi-worker rebalance is what triggers the race).

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

During a Connect rebalance/roll, a connector task's schema-history recovery should complete (or be retried) regardless of whether another instance of the same connector is concurrently recovering. Schema-history recovery is a read of a single-partition internal topic and should not be subject to consumer-group partition contention.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

**Where**: KafkaSchemaHistory.java#L307-L325 ([code](https://github.com/debezium/debezium/blob/main/debezium-storage/debezium-storage-kafka/src/main/java/io/debezium/storage/kafka/history/KafkaSchemaHistory.java#L307-L325)).

Recovery reads the history topic via `historyConsumer.subscribe(...)` (L311), i.e. it joins a consumer group. The `group.id` is set from the schema-history name (L218/L222); under Kafka Connect this is deterministic per connector - so every recovery instance of a given connector joins the same group. The history topic has a single partition.

**The race**: if two recovery instances are ever active concurrently for the same connector, both join that one group, and the single partition is assigned to only one of them via the group protocol. The other member gets an empty assignment (`partitions=[]`), polls zero records, and after `recovery.attempts * recovery.poll.interval.ms` throws:
```
java.lang.IllegalStateException: The database schema history couldn't be recovered.
at io.debezium.storage.kafka.history.KafkaSchemaHistory.recoverRecords(KafkaSchemaHistory.java:325)
```
The task then fails. Concurrent recovery is realistic during a rebalance between multiple workers, where a task can be starting on a new worker while the previous instance is still tearing down (its recovery consumer hasn't left the group yet).

**Proposed fix**:
Use `assign()` instead of `subscribe()` for recovery - pin the consumer directly to the single partition and `seekToBeginning`, joining no group. Recovery is inherently a full read-from-beginning of a 1-partition topic, so group membership /partition distribution buys nothing - it only introduces the contention. With `assign()`, any number of instances can recover independently.

Alternative considered: a unique `group.id` per recovery (UUID suffix) also fixes the race, but spawns a throwaway consumer group. `assign()` avoids that - recovery is just a read-from-beginning of one partition, no coordination needed.

Discussion [here](https://debezium.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/302533-dev/topic/KafkaSchemaHistory.20recovery.20race.20.28subscribe.20vs.20assign.29/with/600601235).

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

_Ideally, also verify with the latest Alpha/Beta/CR version._

Yes - reproduced on 3.5.2.Final . The `subscribe()`-based recovery is also present unchanged on main.

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

_You might be asked later to provide [DEBUG/TRACE](https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/operations/logging.html) level log._

Snippet from a 3-worker run on 3.5.2.Final. During the rebalance, the schema-history recovery consumer for connector `debezium-sqlserver-source` joins the per-connector group `server1-schemahistory` on the single-partition topic `schema-changes.testdb-0`. When two recovery instances overlap, only one wins the partition; the other gets an empty assignment and fails:
```
[2026-06-05 08:47:15,326] INFO [debezium-sqlserver-source|task-0] [Consumer clientId=server1-schemahistory, groupId=server1-schemahistory] Finished assignment for group at generation 28: {server1-schemahistory-9de91bfe-...=Assignment(partitions=[schema-changes.testdb-0])} (o.a.k.c.c.internals.ConsumerCoordinator)
[2026-06-05 08:47:15,328] INFO [debezium-sqlserver-source|task-0] [Consumer clientId=server1-schemahistory, groupId=server1-schemahistory] Notifying assignor about the new Assignment(partitions=[schema-changes.testdb-0]) (o.a.k.c.c.internals.ConsumerCoordinator)
```
The concurrently-recovering instance on another worker is assigned `partitions=[]` for the same group, polls zero records, and after `recovery.attempts * recovery.poll.interval.ms`:
```
[2026-06-05 08:47:38,424] ERROR [debezium-sqlserver-source|task-0] WorkerSourceTask{id=debezium-sqlserver-source-0} Task threw an uncaught and unrecoverable exception. Task is being killed and will not recover until manually restarted (org.apache.kafka.connect.runtime.WorkerTask)
java.lang.IllegalStateException: The database schema history couldn't be recovered. Consider to increase the value for schema.history.internal.kafka.recovery.poll.interval.ms
at io.debezium.storage.kafka.history.KafkaSchemaHistory.recoverRecords(KafkaSchemaHistory.java:325)
at io.debezium.relational.history.AbstractSchemaHistory.recover(AbstractSchemaHistory.java:101)
at io.debezium.relational.history.SchemaHistory.recover(SchemaHistory.java:209)
at io.debezium.relational.HistorizedRelationalDatabaseSchema.recover(HistorizedRelationalDatabaseSchema.java:68)
```
Can share full logs if needed.

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**

Reproduced deterministically with a 3-worker
Connect cluster:
1. SQL Server + Kafka Connect (3 workers), Debezium SQL Server 3.5.2.Final.
2. Create a database with many CDC-enabled tables and start the connector; let the snapshot complete so schema history is populated.
3. (Optional, to widen the window) bloat the history topic so recovery replay takes longer.
4. Set `scheduled.rebalance.max.delay.ms=0` on the workers and bounce the worker holding the task (e.g. restart its container) repeatedly, so the task migrates to another worker while the previous instance is still in the schema-history consumer group.
5. One of the overlapping recovery consumers receives `partitions=[]` and the task fails with "The database schema history couldn't be recovered."
author: 100maygupta

project_fields.Title: KafkaSchemaHistory recovery race (subscribe vs assign)

title: Reorganize Oracle documentation
body:
Currently, the vast majority of the Oracle documentation (including examples, prerequisites, steps) assumes LogMiner. There are subsections specifically dedicated to XStream or OpenLogReplicator. 

I'd like to propose that we transition to top-level sections focused on topics, setup, and examples applicable to all adapters. For sections that differentiate between adapters, we either colocate LogMiner/XStream parts together into the same section or that top-level section supplies links to later sections that are LogMiner, XStream, or OLR specific.

A good example is our connector properties section. There are a number of these that are common across all adapters, while there are ones that are specific to a subset of adapters. It would be easier for ingestion to split these into more adapter-specific sections.

Another option I had thought about was to use partials heavily for duplicate sections that might apply to two of the three adapters, so we can define the content once but list it in adapter-specific sections. It significantly eases the maintenance burden and aligns with better organization by adapters. But I'm not sure what the downstream impact would be.

@roldanbob I would love your feedback/thoughts.
author: Naros

project_fields.Title: Reorganize Oracle documentation

title: Build Week 2: Add source and destination management commands to Debezium JBang CLI
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

As part of the GSoC 2026 project to build a unified Debezium CLI, this issue implements source and destination management commands in the JBang-based CLI.

This follows the pipeline management commands implemented in #2019 and extends the platform subsystem to cover sources and destinations.

Commands to implement:

Sources:
  - dbz source list              - List all sources
  - dbz source get <id>          - Get a source by id
  - dbz source create --file     - Create a source from a YAML file
  - dbz source update <id> --file - Update a source from a YAML file
  - dbz source delete <id>       - Delete a source by id

Destinations:
  - dbz destination list              - List all destinations
  - dbz destination get <id>          - Get a destination by id
  - dbz destination create --file     - Create a destination from a YAML file
  - dbz destination update <id> --file - Update a destination from a YAML file
  - dbz destination delete <id>       - Delete a destination by id

Implementation notes:
  - Follows the same patterns established in DBZ-2019 (pipeline commands)
  - Reuses HttpPlatformClient, PlatformApiMixin, and the platform.dto package
  - New DTOs (SourceRequest, SourceResponse, DestinationRequest, 
    DestinationResponse) will be added to the platform.dto package
  - New HTTP client methods added to PlatformClient interface and 
    HttpPlatformClient implementation

title: Implement Week 3 synchronization layer and Chroma vector store integration
body:
### Description
This tracking issue covers the GSoC Week 3 deliverables for the PyDebeziumAI project. The goal is to implement the core `SyncManager` layer and achieve an end-to-end real-time synchronization pipeline with a Chroma vector database backend.

### Sub-tasks

- [ ] **Sub-task 1: Core SyncManager & CDC Operations Handling**
  - Implement the `SyncManager` to process `DebeziumEventModel` streams.
  - Implement logic for mapping CDC operations:
    - `insert` (c) / `read` (r) -> add
    - `update` (u) -> delete + insert
    - `delete` (d) -> delete or soft-delete fallback.
- [ ] **Sub-task 2: Chroma Backend Integration**
  - Implement `ChromaAdapter` extending the base `VectorStoreAdapter`.
  - Wire the adapter to the `SyncManager`.
- [ ] **Sub-task 3: End-to-End Pipeline & Integration Tests**
  - Ensure the pipeline successfully flows from Ingestion -> DocumentBuilder -> SyncManager -> Chroma.
  - Add end-to-end unit/integration tests to verify the `SyncManager` correctly synchronizes CDC events.
author: KMohnishM

project_fields.Title: Implement Week 3 synchronization layer and Chroma vector store integration

title: PostgreSQL connector slow startup with many custom types
body:
## What happened?
The PostgreSQL connector takes 30+ minutes to start on databases with large numbers of custom types (e.g. databases migrated from Oracle using AWS Schema Conversion Tool generating 30,000+ custom types).

## Root Cause
TypeRegistry.prime() fires individual SQL_OID_LOOKUP queries per unresolved type due to dependency ordering issues caused by PostgreSQL query planner choosing MERGE JOIN for SQL_TYPES.

## Fix
Implemented in PR debezium/debezium#7462

## Debezium version
latest main
author: Vinu2111

project_fields.Title: PostgreSQL connector slow startup with many custom types

title: Allow transaction creation in Oracle to be deferred until first DML occurs
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

**Which use case/requirement will be addressed by the proposed feature?**

In earlier versions of Debezium, this pull request was implemented to defer transaction capture until the first DML event occurs:  https://github.com/debezium/debezium/pull/5252.  This was done by not tracking the START event at all which meant that a transaction was only created once DML was seen.

A side-effect of this was that we would lose track of the source.user_name when a transaction was mined in two steps.  This was addressed in https://github.com/debezium/debezium/pull/6340 which restored the START attribute so that user_name and client_id capture is always guaranteed.  log.mining.buffer.memory.legacy.transaction.start was created as an internal config for users that wanted the old behaviour.

When we implemented this in our environments, a side-effect was that the number of transactions that were tracked greatly increased.  This included "stub" transactions created by frontend Java apps and developers using sql developer.  These are transactions with just a START and no other operations until it is eventually committed or rolled back.  We also increased the likelihood of known edge cases as we now tracked transactions we previously didn't (for example, rollbacks for symbolic links).

In later DBZ versions, the mining process has also changed and so the behaviour of log.mining.buffer.memory.legacy.transaction.start is such that long-running transactions pin the mining window while excluding the transaction start which is the worst of both worlds as you don't resolve edge cases and also unnecessarily pin the mining window.

What I'd like:  Introduce a deferred transaction start configuration that emulates the <= 3.0.8 behaviour but without losing the user_name and client_id metadata.  This could eventually replace the internal legacy.transaction.start configuration.  This would be really useful in a lot of our environments where we currently use legacy.transaction.start (on 3.2.0) but have a requirement for client_id and user_name.


---

**Implementation ideas (optional)**

When a START is seen, don't  immediately create the "real" transaction.  Create a transaction metadata record that stores the required metadata (user_name, client_id etc.).  This metadata record gets promoted to a "real" transaction once DML is seen.  Have additional configuration that allows clean up of these transactions so they don't sit in memory forever ( if e.g. the COMMIT is never seen due to edge case).  This would approximate what the older defer transaction PR was achieving without sacrificing the transaction START.

I have a version of this working that I could submit as a PR.
author: nathan-smit-1

project_fields.Title: Allow transaction creation in Oracle to be deferred until first DML occurs

title: MySQL connector: Snapshot step 6 writes DROP+CREATE for every table to schema history topic, causing unnecessary hours-long Kafka roundtrip
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

MySQL connector.  Version: 3.3.2.Final (confirmed on main/3.5.0 as well)

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

Fast step 6 of initial snapshot.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

During an initial snapshot, step 5 ("Reading structure of captured tables") reads the current schema directly from MySQL -- this completes in minutes. Step 6 ("Persisting schema history") then writes a DROP TABLE IF EXISTS + CREATE TABLE record to the Kafka schema history topic for every table in every captured database.

For example, with 50 databases and 1,000 tables each, step 6 produces ~100K records (2 per table per database). These are written sequentially to a single-partition topic. 

On a distributed Kafka cluster with acks=all and min.insync.replicas=2, each record is written sequentially and must wait for replication acknowledgment before the next is sent. With typical per-record latencies of 5-25ms (depending on cluster load, record size, and serialization overhead), 100K records takes anywhere from 8 minutes to 40+ minutes just for writes. The subsequent recovery replay adds a similar or longer delay due to single-threaded DDL parsing. Combined, a fresh snapshot followed by one restart can cost 1-3 hours of idle time before any data flows.

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**

1. Have a MySQL instance with 1,000+ tables across multiple databases
2. Have a distributed Kafka cluster (3+ brokers, min.insync.replicas=2) with a Debezium MySQL connector
3. Start a fresh snapshot (no prior offsets or schema history topic)
4. Observe step 5 ("Reading structure") completes in minutes
5. Observe step 6 ("Persisting schema history") takes disproportionately longer writing DDL to the schema history topic -- each record incurs full replication latency (acks=all)

**Note**: The problem scales with replication overhead. With acks=all and min.insync.replicas=2 on a distributed Kafka cluster, each of the ~100K schema history records requires a full round-trip to replicate before the next is written. On a single-node Kafka with acks=1, step 6 completes significantly faster.
author: ptqa

project_fields.Title: MySQL connector: Snapshot step 6 writes DROP+CREATE for every table to schema history topic, causing unnecessary hours-long Kafka roundtrip
