title: Neo4j Connector
body:
### Context

Neo4j is the most widely adopted graph database, used across industries for fraud detection, recommendation engines, knowledge graphs, identity and access management, and network/IT operations. 
Organizations running Neo4j often need to propagate graph changes like node and relationship creates,
updates, and deletes, into downstream systems such as data warehouses, search indexes, event-driven microservices, and audit pipelines.

Since version **5.13**, Neo4j Enterprise ships with a built-in Change Data Capture API that exposes a change stream through three Cypher procedures ` (db.cdc.current, db.cdc.earliest, db.cdc.query).` 
This gives Debezium a stable, supported foundation for capturing changes without relying on internal log formats or unsupported interfaces. Today Debezium has no support for graph databases. A Neo4j connector closes that gap and brings Debezium's proven CDC capabilities
  like snapshotting, streaming, offset-based resume, and standard event envelopes, to the graph world.


###   Approach
The connector will live in a standalone repository (_debezium-connector-neo4j_).
Work is divided into phases delivered as sub-issues and pull requests. 
A design document will be submitted to the **_debezium-design-documents_** repository describing the CDC polling strategy, schema mapping decisions, and topic naming conventions.
author: vsantonastaso

project_fields.Title: Neo4j Connector

title: [postgres] Map geometric types (box, circle, line, lseg, path, polygon) to STRING instead of opaque bytea
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

**Which use case/requirement will be addressed by the proposed feature?**

The PostgreSQL connector currently maps only `point` to a proper Connect schema (`io.debezium.data.geometry.Point`). The other built-in geometric types — `box`, `circle`, `line`, `lseg`, `path`, `polygon` — have no mapping in `PostgresValueConverter`, so they fall through to the default branch and are emitted as opaque `bytea` (only when `include.unknown.datatypes=true`; otherwise the column is dropped entirely).

As a result, users who replicate tables containing these common geometric columns cannot get usable values downstream — the data arrives as raw bytes instead of a meaningful representation. This also breaks PostgreSQL → PostgreSQL replication, since the `bytea` form does not re-insert into a geometric column.

This request is to give these six types a first-class mapping so their values are propagated in a usable form, consistent with the other PostgreSQL-specific types the connector already supports.

**Environment:** Debezium 3.6.0-SNAPSHOT (`main`), PostgreSQL connector, `pgoutput` decoder.

---

**Implementation ideas (optional)**

Value extraction is **already implemented**: `ReplicationMessageColumnValueResolver` (lines 128–145) already returns `PGbox` / `PGcircle` / `PGline` / `PGlseg` / `PGpath` / `PGpolygon`. A maintainer comment right above (line 126) even notes these were intentionally left without a schema converter:

> note that, with the exception of point, no converters for these types are implemented yet

Only the schema/converter mapping is missing. The proposal is to map all six types to `SchemaBuilder.string()`, preserving the canonical PostgreSQL text representation via `PGobject.getValue()` (every geometric `PGxxx` class extends `org.postgresql.util.PGobject`). The conversion can reuse the existing `convertCitext()` pattern verbatim.

This mirrors how other PostgreSQL-specific composite types are already handled — `inet`, `cidr`, `macaddr`, `macaddr8`, `tsrange`, `tstzrange`, `daterange`, `int4range`, `int8range`, `numrange` all map to STRING. It keeps the change scoped to a single converter file (plus tests and docs).

I considered STRUCT-style mapping like `point`, but the six types have heterogeneous shapes (box/lseg = 2 points, circle = center + radius, line = 3 coefficients, path/polygon = variable-length point lists), so STRING is the most consistent and lowest-risk option. Happy to switch to STRUCT if maintainers prefer.
author: sundongkim-dev

project_fields.Title: [postgres] Map geometric types (box, circle, line, lseg, path, polygon) to STRING instead of opaque bytea

---

_E.g. on-premises, with a specific cloud provider, etc._

`Postgres 14.22 on AWS`

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

`For a PSQLException exception such as "PSQLException: FATAL: role not permitted to log in" that the task status will eventually be set to FAILED.`

---

**What behavior do you see?**

`What we see instead is that the task is forever retrying and the status remains as 'RUNNING' which defeats our existing infrastructure to recover from failed tasks. It does not believe that there's anything to recover if the task's status is 'RUNNING'`

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

`<Your answer>`

_You might be asked later to provide [DEBUG/TRACE](https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/operations/logging.html) level log._

`<Your answer>`

title: Simplify Prometheus url setting in Debezium Platform
body:
The conductor's `application.yml` declares a REST client for Prometheus:

````yaml 
prometheus-api:
   url: ${MONITORING_PROMETHEUS_URL:http://localhost:9090}
````

This env var is never set in the Helm deployment template, so the conductor always falls back to localhost:9090 if the users does not explicitly set the correct env. 
The Helm `values.yaml` has `monitoring.prometheus.external.url` but it's never wired to anything.
author: mfvitale

project_fields.Title: Simplify Prometheus url setting in Debezium Platform

title: Build Week 4: Add pipeline logs and catalog commands to Debezium JBang CLI
body:
This GSoC 2026 project task proposes extending the Debezium CLI with pipeline log access and catalog discovery commands, building on the previously implemented pipeline, source, destination, connection, and transform commands.

**Proposed Pipeline Logs Commands:**
- `debezium pipeline logs <id>` - download the full log dump for a pipeline
- `debezium pipeline logs --stream <id>` - live tail pipeline logs over WebSocket (the `kubectl logs -f` equivalent for Debezium)

**Proposed Catalog Commands:**
- `debezium catalog list [--type <type>]` - list available connector/component descriptors for the current platform version
- `debezium catalog get <type> <class>` - get the descriptor for a specific component

The implementation will reuse the existing `PlatformFactory`/`*Service` pattern (adding a `pipelineLogs()`/`catalog()` method to the shared `PlatformFactory`) and the per-feature `platform.<feature>.{dto,mapper,service,api}` package layout established in prior weeks. Catalog responses have no fixed schema on the backend (`CatalogService` reads/filters raw `manifest.json`/descriptor files), so the catalog DTOs will be modeled generically rather than as typed records. The log streaming command is new infrastructure relative to prior weeks: it requires a `quarkus-websockets-next` WebSocket client connecting to `/api/pipelines/{id}/logs/stream`, rather than a plain REST call. Integration tests will use Testcontainers with the real `quay.io/debezium/platform-conductor:nightly` image, following the `jbang/*JB` + `nativebuild/*IT` test split.
author: Binayak490-cyber

project_fields.Title: Build Week 4: Add pipeline logs and catalog commands to Debezium JBang CLI

title: Milvus Source Connector: Packaging and Dispatching for the streaming pipeline
body:
## Description

Replace the stub connector that discarded events at log with a full Debezium pipeline. The streaming source should deserializes Milvus protobuf from Kafka, orders events by TSO, and dispatch real SourceRecords through the EventDispatcher into the ChangeEventQueue for consumption. 

Integration Test to verify the Streaming Pipelines CDC events being streamed.

### Components

- MilvusEventDispatcher
- MilvusEventMetadataProvider
- MilvusSnapshotter
- StreamingPipelineIT

## Goals
- Wire dispatching instead of logging at DEBUG
- Build the ChangeEventSourceCoordinator chain so the connector actually runs and produces records
- Register collection schemas dynamically from Insert event data so RelationalChangeRecordEmitter can build Structs

title: Support running the Spanner source connector without a Kafka broker
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

_For feature requests or enhancements, please provide the following information:_

**Which use case/requirement will be addressed by the proposed feature?**

While most Debezium source connectors can run in the embedded engine / Debezium Server without a Kafka broker, the Spanner source connector currently requires one. It uses Kafka internally for cross task coordination and low watermark aggregation which makes it hard to use in broker-less deployments.

We've been exploring making it runnable without a broker for the single task case, as with one task there's no cross task state to share, so neither the coordination nor the low watermark aggregation needs a broker.

The approach pulls the Kafka-coupled coordination behind a small SPI. This keeps the existing Kafka path as the default (multi-task, unchanged) and adds a broker-less single task implementation (as a nice side effect this turns coordination into a pluggable extension point, so others could provide their own backend instead of Kafka). The mode is chosen by a simple config flag.

We have a working PoC but before going further I wanted to ask: would the maintainers be open to upstreaming something like this?

Happy to share the design, the SPI, and more details around the same.
author: ahanumeswarakulkarni

project_fields.Title: Support running the Spanner source connector without a Kafka broker

title: Add SingleStore support to the Debezium JDBC sink connector
body:
### Description

SingleStore is a distributed SQL database that is compatible with the MySQL wire protocol, but it has SQL syntax and type-handling differences from MySQL/MariaDB. I would like to add first-class SingleStore support to the Debezium JDBC sink connector through a dedicated dialect and test coverage.

This was previously discussed with the Debezium community. The proposed direction was to add a standalone dialect, selected for SingleStore connections, with integration and end-to-end tests.

### Proposed changes

- Add a SingleStore JDBC sink dialect.
- Add dialect/provider registration for SingleStore.
- Add the SingleStore JDBC driver dependency, using the latest stable driver: `com.singlestore:singlestore-jdbc-client:1.2.11`.
- Add SingleStore integration tests using `ghcr.io/singlestore-labs/singlestoredb-dev:0.2.77` / SingleStoreDB 9.0.23.
- Add an end-to-end pipeline test for a Debezium source writing to a SingleStore sink.
- Update JDBC sink connector documentation.

The initial scope would not include Debezium geospatial logical type support for SingleStore. SingleStore geospatial types and binding semantics differ from MySQL/MariaDB, so that should be validated separately before being enabled.

### Test image and licensing

SingleStore provides the SingleStore Dev Image for local development and CI-style functional testing. The image does not require a license key for development, prototyping, and functional testing, but it is not intended for production workloads or benchmarks.
author: minleejae

project_fields.Title: Add SingleStore support to the Debezium JDBC sink connector

title: CockroachDB: TIMETZ/TIMESTAMPTZ emitted as schema-invalid strings fail ISO_OFFSET parsing in downstream sinks
body:
## Problem

After dbz#2083 mapped `TIMESTAMPTZ` to `io.debezium.time.ZonedTimestamp` and `TIMETZ` to `io.debezium.time.ZonedTime`, the connector emits the CockroachDB changefeed value unchanged (passthrough). Those Debezium logical types require an ISO-8601 value that carries an offset: `ZonedTimestamp` uses `DateTimeFormatter.ISO_OFFSET_DATE_TIME` and `ZonedTime` uses `DateTimeFormatter.ISO_OFFSET_TIME`. The passthrough value does not always satisfy that contract:

- A `TIMETZ` value is emitted by CockroachDB with an hour-only `+HH` offset (for example `14:36:34.873+00`). `ISO_OFFSET_TIME` does not accept an hour-only offset, so every `TIMETZ` value fails to parse downstream.
- A zoneless value carrying the `ZonedTimestamp`/`ZonedTime` schema (for example an old row replayed after a column was altered between `TIMESTAMP` and `TIMESTAMPTZ`) fails because no offset is present.

A downstream Debezium JDBC sink fails:

```
java.time.format.DateTimeParseException: Text '2026-06-15T14:36:34.873' could not be parsed at index 23
  at io.debezium.connector.jdbc.JdbcChangeEventSink...
```

## Evidence

CockroachDB v25.4.10 enriched changefeed values:

- `TIMESTAMP` -> `2026-06-15T14:36:34.873` (zoneless)
- `TIMESTAMPTZ` -> `2026-06-15T14:36:34.873Z`
- `TIME` -> `14:36:34.873`
- `TIMETZ` -> `14:36:34.873+00`

`OffsetTime.parse("14:36:34.873+00", ISO_OFFSET_TIME)` throws at index 12, and `OffsetDateTime.parse("2026-06-15T14:36:34.873", ISO_OFFSET_DATE_TIME)` throws at index 23.

## Fix

Normalize the value in the connector so the emitted string always satisfies the logical type formatter: parse the CockroachDB value (offset `Z`, `+HH`, or `+HH:MM`, or zoneless interpreted as UTC) and re-emit using `ISO_OFFSET_DATE_TIME` (`ZonedTimestamp`) and `ISO_OFFSET_TIME` (`ZonedTime`). Apply in both the change-record emitter and the value converter.

## Verification

- Unit tests on the emitter and value converter for `Z`, hour-only offset, `+HH:MM` offset, and zoneless inputs, asserting the output parses with the logical type formatter.
- Extend `CockroachDBTemporalTypesIT` to assert `tstz`/`tmtz` values parse with `ISO_OFFSET_DATE_TIME`/`ISO_OFFSET_TIME`.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDB: TIMETZ/TIMESTAMPTZ emitted as schema-invalid strings fail ISO_OFFSET parsing in downstream sinks

## Bug report

`

We have recently migrated our Dev environment from Debezium 3.5.0.Final to 3.6.0.CR, Kafka 4.1.2 to 4.3.0.  After making the switch, we're seeing the following warning thrown on the initial snapshot of the table PROMODFQ.TFPCH:

```
[ ... ]
Caused by: org.apache.kafka.connect.errors.DataException: Invalid value: null used for required field: "PCCHGDATE", schema type: INT64      
        at org.apache.kafka.connect.data.ConnectSchema.validateValue(ConnectSchema.java:224)
        at org.apache.kafka.connect.data.ConnectSchema.validateValue(ConnectSchema.java:217)
        at org.apache.kafka.connect.data.Struct.validate(Struct.java:233)
        at org.apache.kafka.connect.data.ConnectSchema.validateValue(ConnectSchema.java:254)
        at org.apache.kafka.connect.data.ConnectSchema.validateValue(ConnectSchema.java:217)
        at org.apache.kafka.connect.data.Struct.put(Struct.java:216)
        at org.apache.kafka.connect.data.Struct.put(Struct.java:203)
        at io.debezium.data.Envelope.read(Envelope.java:260)

```

This is easily reproducible using full, clean redeployments of the stack between 3.5.0 and 3.6.0.CR1, with the former not suffering this error.  This is seen with both Kafka 4.1.2 and 4.3.0.  We've repeated the clean redeployment test between the two DBZ versions three times with the same results each time.  Logfile from the 3.6.0.CR1 failure is attached.

The relevant DDL for the table in question is:
```
CREATE TABLE PROMODFQ.TFPCH (
[ ... ]
	PCCHGDATE TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP NOT NULL
);
```
This schema and the underlying data does not vary between tests.

title: CockroachDB: connector re-emits the entire intermediate changefeed topic on every restart (consumer offset never committed)
body:
## Problem

In kafka mode the connector consumes the intermediate changefeed topic with a KafkaConsumer that has `enable.auto.commit=false` and never calls `commitSync`/`commitAsync`/`seek`. The consumer group is stable (`cockroachdb-connector`) and `auto.offset.reset` defaults to `earliest`. Because no offset is ever committed, every restart resets to `earliest` and re-reads the entire retained intermediate topic. The deduplication is an in-memory bounded LRU cache, so it is empty after a restart and does not suppress the replay.

Net effect: every source connector restart re-emits its whole retained backlog to the output topics. The CockroachDB cursor stored in `connect-offsets` controls only what the changefeed emits; it does not prevent the consumer from re-reading already-processed records.

## Impact

At-least-once is preserved end to end when the downstream sink is idempotent (upsert), so the target data stays correct. But the output topics are amplified by a full backlog replay on each restart, which causes reprocessing load, downstream lag, and topic bloat proportional to retention and restart count.

## Evidence

A reproduction IT drives 5 inserts through the connector, then reads the consumer group's committed offsets with an admin client:

```
Committed consumer-group offset total for group cockroachdb-connector: 0
```

No offset is ever committed, so a restart resets to `earliest`.

## Fix direction

Commit the intermediate consumer offset, tied to the Debezium offset flush so at-least-once is preserved (no data loss on crash), so a restart resumes from the committed position instead of replaying the whole topic. The sinkless mode removes the intermediate consumer entirely and is not affected.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDB: connector re-emits the entire intermediate changefeed topic on every restart (consumer offset never committed)

title: CockroachDB: drive the snapshot lifecycle and metrics for the changefeed initial scan
body:
## Problem

The connector performs the initial backfill through the CockroachDB changefeed `initial_scan` in the streaming path, and the framework snapshot source returns `skipped`. As a result the standard Debezium snapshot lifecycle is never driven, so several surfaces report incorrect or empty snapshot state, unlike the PostgreSQL and other connectors which drive it through `CommonOffsetContext` (`preSnapshotStart` / `preSnapshotCompletion` / `postSnapshotCompletion`) and `SnapshotProgressListener`:

- The offset key `snapshot_completed` is never set to `true`, so `GET /connectors/<name>/offsets` always shows `false` even after the backfill finishes.
- The `source.snapshot` field on the backfill records is never set, so initial-scan reads (`op=r`) do not carry `snapshot=true`/`last`.
- The JMX snapshot-metrics MBean (`SnapshotRunning`, `SnapshotCompleted`, ...) is never updated, so Prometheus/Grafana cannot observe snapshot progress or completion.

The connector already detects the exact boundaries internally (`initialScanInProgress` is set when the changefeed is created with `initial_scan='yes'`, and cleared on the first resolved timestamp), so the state is available; it is just not propagated.

## Change

Drive the standard snapshot lifecycle from the changefeed initial scan, matching the core convention:

- At initial-scan start: `preSnapshotStart(false)` and `SnapshotProgressListener.snapshotStarted(partition)`.
- On backfill reads: `markSnapshotRecord(SnapshotRecord.TRUE)` so `source.snapshot` is set.
- At the first resolved timestamp (scan complete): `preSnapshotCompletion()`, `postSnapshotCompletion()`, and `SnapshotProgressListener.snapshotCompleted(partition)`.
- For modes with no initial scan and for restarts with a prior cursor: mark the snapshot completed up front.

Also clarify the consumer resume log message to print the last committed offset and the next offset being sought (it previously printed the seek position under a "stored offset" label).

## Verification

- Unit tests for the offset `snapshot_completed` transitions.
- An IT asserting that initial-scan reads carry `source.snapshot=true`, `snapshot_completed` is `true` after the first resolved timestamp, and steady-state records carry `snapshot=false`.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: CockroachDB: drive the snapshot lifecycle and metrics for the changefeed initial scan

title: Week 6: Implement Structured Metadata Filtering & LangGraph Reactive Agent
body:
### Description
This issue tracks the Week 6 milestone for the PyDebeziumAI GSoC 2026 project. Following the implementation and verification of our core vector store adapters (Chroma, PGVector, Milvus), this milestone focuses on standardizing search query capabilities across different backends using structured metadata filtering and demonstrating a reactive LangGraph agent example.

We will introduce a unified metadata filtering API for all adapters, translating standard Python filter dictionaries dynamically to backend-specific filter formats. Additionally, we will construct a second complete example application demonstrating a reactive LangGraph agent whose decisions adapt in real-time to change data capture (CDC) updates.

### Goals
- **Structured Metadata Filtering**: Define and implement a uniform metadata filtering contract across all adapters, enabling developers to pass a single dict-based filter syntax that gets translated to the target backend's native query format.
- **Reactive LangGraph Agent Example**: Build a second runnable example showcasing a LangGraph agent utilizing retriever tools backed by PyDebeziumAI, illustrating real-time context updates and reactive decision adjustments as underlying database rows change.
- **Testing**: Extend the unit and integration test suites to verify that metadata filters correctly restrict search queries across Chroma, PGVector, and Milvus.

### Implementation Sub-tasks 
To maintain a clean and modular review process, the objectives will be split into two stacked PRs. You can track their individual sub-tasks below:

- [ ] **Sub-task 1: Unified Metadata Filtering** 
  - Modify `as_retriever` signature in `VectorStoreAdapter` (base.py) to accept an optional `metadata_filter: dict[str, Any]` parameter.
  - Implement translation of `metadata_filter` dict to native dictionary filters in `ChromaAdapter` and `PGVectorAdapter`.
  - Implement translation of `metadata_filter` dict to boolean expression strings (e.g. `(table_name == "products") and (price > 100)`) in `MilvusAdapter`.
  - Add filtering tests to `test_chroma_pipeline.py`, `test_milvus_adapter.py`, and `test_pgvector_adapter.py`.

- [ ] **Sub-task 2: Reactive LangGraph Agent Demo** 
  - Create the `examples/reactive_agent/` directory.
  - Implement a LangGraph agent (`examples/reactive_agent/agent.py`) representing an inventory or support assistant.
  - Build the real-time sync worker (`examples/reactive_agent/stream_sync.py`) running embedded Debezium to sync changes.
  - Write `demo.py` demonstrating how database updates trigger real-time WAL capture, indexing, and immediate agent response modifications.
  - Provide complete setup and run instructions in `README.md`.
author: KMohnishM

project_fields.Title: Week 6: Implement Structured Metadata Filtering & LangGraph Reactive Agent

title: Stream ongoing changes from the CDC log with a clean snapshot handoff
body:
Stream ongoing changes from `_debezium_cdc_log` and hand off from the snapshot with no gaps and no
duplicates. This is the SQLite connector's first live change data capture.

This is part of the SQLite connector tracked in issue #2056. It builds on the config, schema, and
snapshot work in #2069.

## What this adds

The connector already boots, loads the schema, and takes an initial snapshot that records a high-water
mark. This issue makes it stream. After the snapshot, the connector polls `_debezium_cdc_log` for new
rows and emits each one as a standard Debezium change event:

- A poll loop reads new rows in `change_id` order, in small bounded batches, and sleeps between polls
  when there is nothing new. Each query opens and closes a short read transaction so it does not block
  SQLite from checkpointing the WAL.
- Each row becomes a `c`, `u`, or `d` change event. The before and after row data stored as JSON in the
  log is decoded into the column structure the connector already knows from the schema, and the framework
  builds the key, value, and envelope.
- The offset advances after every row, so a stop part way through a batch re-reads only the rows after
  the last one emitted.
- The `source` block of each event carries the change position and the commit time: `ts_ms` comes from
  the row's `committed_at`, and the source metadata exposes the `change_id` and the table name.

## The handoff

The main correctness goal is a clean handoff from snapshot to streaming. While the snapshot reads the
tables, the generator keeps writing new rows to the log. The snapshot records the largest `change_id`
visible inside its read view as a high-water mark, and streaming resumes at the first `change_id` past
it. Every change committed after the snapshot's view opened has a larger id, so it is absent from the
snapshot data and delivered once by streaming. No change is missed and none is delivered twice.

## Scope

- This issue covers the poll loop, the change event emitter, and the handoff from the snapshot to
  streaming.
- The work is tested against the SQL trigger stand-in, which writes the same `_debezium_cdc_log` shape
  the native extension will write, so the streaming code does not depend on which generator is in use.
  The integration tests run against a temporary database file with no Docker.

## Done when

- A live insert, update, and delete each arrive as the matching change event with the right before and
  after values and source metadata.
- A write committed during the snapshot window is delivered by streaming exactly once.
- `./mvnw verify` is green, unit and integration.
author: siddhantcvdi

project_fields.Title: Stream ongoing changes from the CDC log with a clean snapshot handoff

title: Add the SQL trigger generator that fills the CDC log
body:
Add the generator that fills `_debezium_cdc_log` using SQL triggers installed by the connector, so the connector captures changes on any stock SQLite database without a native extension or writer-side setup.

This is part of the SQLite connector tracked in issue #2056. The connector already reads `_debezium_cdc_log` and streams changes from it. This issue makes the trigger generator the real v1 generator, not just a development stand-in.

## Why triggers

The original plan was a native C extension using SQLite's preupdate hook, shipped as prebuilt binaries. De-risking that plan showed it does not work: the preupdate hook is not part of SQLite's loadable-extension API, so it cannot be delivered as a loadable `.so`/`.dylib`/`.dll`. It only exists when SQLite itself is compiled with `SQLITE_ENABLE_PREUPDATE_HOOK`, which would force every writer to adopt a custom SQLite build. That build also only captures writers that use it, so a write from a stock `sqlite3` client would be missed. For SQLite's usual home (edge, mobile, IoT, local-first apps, many languages) that cost is too high.

Triggers avoid all of it. They live in the database file, so they fire for every writer in every language, and the connector installs them over its normal JDBC connection. This is the approach every production SQLite CDC system uses (kevinconway/sqlite-cdc, audite, sqlite-history, cr-sqlite, ElectricSQL, PowerSync).

## What it does

For each monitored table the connector installs `AFTER INSERT`, `AFTER UPDATE`, and `AFTER DELETE` triggers. Each trigger writes one row into `_debezium_cdc_log` with the table name, the operation (`c`, `u`, or `d`), the before and after row images as JSON, and a commit timestamp, ordered by a monotonic `change_id`. The column list is baked into the trigger from `PRAGMA table_info` when it is installed.

Two things the naive trigger pattern gets wrong, which this generator handles:

- **Lossless values including blobs.** A plain `json_object` mangles raw bytes. Values are tagged by `typeof` and blobs are emitted as hex, so every storage class round-trips exactly.
- **Wide tables.** `json_object` caps out around 63 arguments. Wide rows are serialized in chunks and merged with `json_patch`, the same technique kevinconway/sqlite-cdc uses.

## Schema changes are out of scope here

A trigger is bound to the column set it was built with, so a write that races an `ALTER TABLE` can be captured with a stale column set. Detecting that, correcting the affected row, and rebuilding the triggers is schema change handling, which is Phase 5 (issue to follow). This issue installs the triggers and captures values. It does not detect schema changes, stamp the row with a column count, or rebuild on drift.

## The contract

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _debezium_cdc_log (
    change_id    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    table_name   TEXT    NOT NULL,
    operation    TEXT    NOT NULL,   -- 'c' | 'u' | 'd'
    old_row_data TEXT,               -- JSON; NULL for INSERT
    new_row_data TEXT,               -- JSON; NULL for DELETE
    committed_at INTEGER NOT NULL    -- unix epoch millis
);
```

## Scope

- The trigger generator that installs the per-table triggers.
- Lossless capture of all five storage classes, including byte-for-byte blobs.
- Wide-table serialization past the `json_object` argument limit.
- Integration tests covering insert/update/delete ordering, blob round-trip, and a wide table.

## Done when

- The installed triggers capture inserts, updates, and deletes into `_debezium_cdc_log` with the right operation, before and after JSON, and ordering.
- All five storage classes round-trip, including blobs.
- A wide table (past the `json_object` limit) serializes correctly.
- `./mvnw verify` is green.
author: siddhantcvdi

project_fields.Title: Add the SQL trigger generator that fills the CDC log

title: DB2Connection - timestampOfLSN returns database current timestamp
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

`Issue noticed in production using 3.2, but the code is the same for this in 3.6`

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

`Not important for this consideration, though the issue appears to be different when running ZOS mode, where a SQL error occurs, while in LUW mode it just returns what appears to be a different result than would be expected.`

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

_E.g. on-premises, with a specific cloud provider, etc._

`Both a DB2 LUW container and DB2 13 running on a Z16.`

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

`DB2Connection has a method called timestampOfLSN(LSN).  The expectation would be that for a given LSN, it should return a matching instant.`

---

**What behavior do you see?**

`It just returns the current timestamp of the database unless the LSN is all-zero or null.  The query doesn't seem to make much sense at all: SELECT CURRENT TIMEstamp FROM sysibm.sysdummy1  WHERE ? > X'00000000000000000000000000000000'`

This is almost always going to exectute as SELECT CURRENT TIMESTAMP FROM sysibm.sysdumm1 WHERE TRUE.

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

_Ideally, also verify with the latest Alpha/Beta/CR version._

`Yes.`

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

_You might be asked later to provide [DEBUG/TRACE](https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/stable/operations/logging.html) level log._

`N/A, this is a logical issue, though I may add logs against Z when I'm able to reproduce there in my lab environment.`

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**

`This is a logical issue, a review of the code and how that query will run should be the easiest way to consider it.`

title: Remove dead code around throwUnexpectedValue in JDBC sink
body:
### Component
debezium-connector-jdbc (JDBC sink)

### Description
`AbstractType.throwUnexpectedValue(Object)` is declared `void` but always throws a `ConnectException`. Because the compiler cannot know the method never returns, every value-returning caller has to append an unreachable `return null;`:

- `AbstractType.requireStruct`
- `ConnectDecimalType.getDefaultValueBinding`
- `dialect/mysql/BigIntUnsignedType`
- `dialect/mysql/BitType.toBitString`

In `BitType.toBitString` the `return null;` sits inside an `if` block that is followed by a normal `return`, so it is dead code that is not even required by the compiler.

### Suggested change
Replace the throwing helper with an exception factory that returns the exception, and let callers throw it:

```java
protected ConnectException unexpectedValue(Object value) {
    return new ConnectException(...);
}
```

Callers use `throw unexpectedValue(value);`. The `throw` statement makes termination explicit to the compiler, removing the four unreachable `return null;` statements. Behavior is unchanged (the same `ConnectException` is thrown).

PR: debezium/debezium#7586
author: minleejae

project_fields.Title: Remove dead code around throwUnexpectedValue in JDBC sink

title: Allow pinning the mining session window using only promoted transactions in deferred mode
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

In 3.6 we have added a configuration that allows for "deferred" transaction start.  The transaction metadata is stored in a map and only promoted to a true transaction once DML is observed.  As part of that change, the mining session window is never pinned as we were looking to emulate the behavior of legacy.transaction.start from earlier DBZ versions.

After this, Chris suggested we investigate an enhancement to this where we emulate the default "pinning" behaviour where the mining session gets pinned but only by transactions that are promoted.  This is a potential middle-ground where users can avoid edge-cases caused by not mining the entire transaction in one pass but in a narrow way that only involves transactions that are "important."   I have been doing some work on this and I'm busy doing testing at the moment.


---

**Implementation ideas (optional)**

Core of the idea is:  Pin the mining session using the eldest transaction in the transaction cache.  When a transaction is promoted from the deferred cache, it will then pin the window.

Caveat, in my current work on this, I have done the following:  When a transaction is promoted and its START_SCN is older than eldest_scn in the transaction cache, the mining session window widens.  However, I am also currently expanding the fetch query window and re-mining that segment in its entirety.  The concern being that in the pass where you retrieve the DML there is some edge-case (missing commit, unsupported operation etc) and we need to remine that segment using the full session window in order to mine correctly.  The reason work to handle recent duplicates should help with this but I'm still thinking through implications
author: nathan-smit-1

project_fields.Title: Allow pinning the mining session window using only promoted transactions in deferred mode

title: Add Read Event Metric in Debezium Core
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

When monitoring a Debezium pipeline through the Platform UI or Prometheus, the snapshot event count panel shows `create=0`, `update=0`, `delete=0` during a snapshot,  even though events are actively being processed. 
This is because snapshot events use `Operation.READ`, which has no dedicated JMX MBean counter.

The `CommonEventMeter` tracks `TotalNumberOfCreateEventsSeen`, `TotalNumberOfUpdateEventsSeen`, and `TotalNumberOfDeleteEventsSeen` individually, but `READ` operations fall into a `default: break` in the switch statement with no counter. 
The only counter that increments during snapshot is `TotalNumberOfEventsSeen`, which is a superset (total = read + create + update + delete) and cannot be used to isolate read events without subtraction and, during streaming, it would double-count.

This makes snapshot event throughput invisible in monitoring dashboards. The OTel JMX config (`debezium-jmx-config.yaml`) cannot map a `read` event type because no MBean attribute exists for it.


---

**Implementation ideas (optional)**

Add a `TotalNumberOfReadEventsSeen` counter to the existing event metrics, following the same pattern as create/update/delete:

1. **`CommonEventMetricsMXBean.java`** — add `long getTotalNumberOfReadEventsSeen()`

2. **`CommonEventMeter.java`** — add counter and handle `READ` in the switch:
   ```java
   protected final AtomicLong totalNumberOfReadEventsSeen = new AtomicLong();
   ```
   ```java
   switch (operation) {
       case CREATE: totalNumberOfCreateEventsSeen.incrementAndGet(); break;
       case UPDATE: totalNumberOfUpdateEventsSeen.incrementAndGet(); break;
       case DELETE: totalNumberOfDeleteEventsSeen.incrementAndGet(); break;
       case READ:   totalNumberOfReadEventsSeen.incrementAndGet(); break;
       default: break;
   }
   ```
   Add getter and include in `reset()`.

3. **OTel JMX config (`debezium-jmx-config.yaml`)**, add mapping in each rule:
   ```yaml
   TotalNumberOfReadEventsSeen:
     metric: event.count
     type: counter
     unit: "{event}"
     desc: The number of change data capture events processed.
     metricAttribute:
       debezium.event.type: const(read)
   ```
4. Add the panel configuration to enable it on Debezium Platform 
   ```yaml
   id: snapshot-event-count
    title: "Snapshot Event Count Rate"
    description: "Rate of snapshot change data capture events per second"
    category: snapshot
    query: 'rate(debezium_event_count_total{service_name="{{pipeline_id}}",debezium_context="snapshot", debezium_event_type="read"}[5m])'
    unit: events/s
    visualization:
      type: area
      suggestedStep: 15s
   ```
This change is backward compatible, it adds a new MBean attribute without modifying existing ones. The `Operation` enum already has a `READ` value; it is just not handled in the switch. The JMX Prometheus exporter (`jmx_prometheus_javaagent`) would also pick up the new attribute if configured.

**Source files:**
- `io.debezium.pipeline.meters.CommonEventMeter`
- `io.debezium.pipeline.metrics.traits.CommonEventMetricsMXBean`
- `io.debezium.pipeline.metrics.SnapshotChangeEventSourceMetricsMXBean` (inherits from `CommonEventMetricsMXBean`)
- Operator: `debezium-jmx-config.yaml` generated by `ConfigMapDependent`

**Limitation**
The `CommonEventMeter` is using by the initial and incremental snapshot. That counter will include both. 
Given that the two snapshot never executes together in the same time frame it is a acceptable.
author: mfvitale

project_fields.Title: Add Read Event Metric in Debezium Core

title: Improve OTel Metrics Latency in Debezium Platform
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

When monitoring a Debezium pipeline through the Debezium Platform, metrics appear with a significant delay (up to ~90 seconds worst case). This makes real-time observation of snapshot progress, queue utilization, and event throughput impractical, by the time the metric is visible, the state may have already changed.

The delay is caused by two OTel environment variables on the Debezium Server pod that default to conservative values:

1. **`OTEL_JMX_INTERVAL_MILLISECONDS`** (default: 10,000ms / 10s) — controls how often the OTel JMX Metric Insight module discovers and scrapes JMX MBeans. With a 10s interval, short-lived snapshots can complete before their MBeans are ever discovered.

2. **`OTEL_METRIC_EXPORT_INTERVAL`** (default: 60,000ms / 60s) — controls how often the OTel Java agent SDK pushes collected metrics to the OTel Collector via OTLP. This is the **single largest contributor** to perceived delay. Even with a 1s JMX scrape, metric values sit in the SDK's in-memory reader for up to 60s before being exported.

The full metrics pipeline has 5 stages, each adding delay:

```
Debezium Server (JMX MBeans)
     │
     ▼ (1) OTel JMX Agent scrapes MBeans        — default 10s
     │
     ▼ (2) OTel SDK exports metrics via OTLP     — default 60s ◄── biggest bottleneck
     │
     ▼ (3) OTel Collector batch processor         — configured 5s
     │
     ▼ (4) OTel Collector Prometheus exporter     — immediate
     │
     ▼ (5) Prometheus scrapes the Collector       — configured 15s
```

Worst-case end-to-end with defaults: **10s + 60s + 5s + 0s + 15s = ~90 seconds**.

Currently these values can only be tuned by manually patching the `DebeziumServer` CR's `spec.runtime.environment.vars`, which gets **lost** when the conductor regenerates the CR on pipeline update.


**Implementation ideas (optional)**

The platform conductor should automatically inject sensible defaults for these env vars when OTel is enabled, so they are preserved across pipeline redeployments.

Proposed defaults:
- `OTEL_JMX_INTERVAL_MILLISECONDS=1000` (1s) : ensures MBeans are discovered within 1 second of registration
- `OTEL_METRIC_EXPORT_INTERVAL=5000` (5s) : reduces the export bottleneck from 60s to 5s

This brings worst-case latency from **~90s to ~26s**.

These could be exposed as platform-level configuration properties under `pipeline.monitoring.otel`:

```yaml
pipeline:
  monitoring:
    otel:
      jmx-interval-ms: 1000
      metric-export-interval-ms: 5000
```

Making them configurable allows operators to tune the trade-off between latency and resource usage (lower intervals mean more OTLP traffic and higher CPU on the collector).

**Additional context:**

There is also a fundamental limitation with JMX-based metric discovery: Debezium's MBeans for snapshot metrics are only **registered** when the snapshot/streaming context starts. The OTel JMX agent cannot scrape them before they exist. For very fast snapshots (< 5s), the snapshot may still complete before the first metric export reaches Prometheus. This would require a Debezium core change to pre-register MBeans before the engine starts.
author: mfvitale

project_fields.Title: Improve OTel Metrics Latency in Debezium Platform

title: MySQL connector: INTEGER UNSIGNED synonym maps to INT32 instead of INT64, diverging from snapshot
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

Debezium MySQL connector, v1.9.0.Final. Also confirmed present on `main` (3.7.0-SNAPSHOT) and reproducible on 2.1.2, 3.1.2.

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

Standard MySQL connector config; not specific to any custom setting.

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

MySQL 8.0 (also reproducible on 5.7). Not deployment-specific.

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

A column declared `INTEGER UNSIGNED` (the `INT` synonym) should map to the same Kafka Connect schema type as `INT UNSIGNED`, `INT64` consistently across snapshot and streaming.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

During streaming, `INTEGER UNSIGNED` columns are mapped to `INT32`; during snapshot (JDBC metadata normalizes `INTEGER` to `INT`), the same column maps to `INT64`. This schema mismatch causes `ClassCastException: java.lang.Integer cannot be cast to class java.lang.Long` downstream.

Root cause: `BinlogValueConverters.schemaBuilder()` / `.converter()` in `debezium-connector-binlog` only match the literal strings `"INT UNSIGNED"` / `"INT4 UNSIGNED"`, `"INTEGER UNSIGNED"` is missing, so it falls through to `JdbcValueConverters`, which maps `Types.INTEGER` → `int32`.

---

**How to reproduce the issue?**

Create a table with an `INTEGER UNSIGNED` column, stream via CDC, and compare its Connect column captured via a full snapshot.

---

I've root-caused this and will submit a fix PR shortly.
author: prathit06

project_fields.Title: MySQL connector: INTEGER UNSIGNED synonym maps to INT32 instead of INT64, diverging from snapshot

title: Chunked snapshot fails with SQL syntax error for tables whose names require quoting (rowCountForTableChunked uses unquoted identifier)
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

PostgreSQL Connector (via Debezium Engine)
Version: 3.5.2.Final (regression introduced in 3.5; not present in 3.4)

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

Standard PostgreSQL connector configuration with parallel snapshot enabled (`snapshot.max.threads > 1`):

```json
{
  "connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.postgresql.PostgresConnector",
  "plugin.name": "pgoutput",
  "database.hostname": "<YOUR_DB_HOST>",
  "database.port": "5432",
  "database.user": "<YOUR_USER>",
  "database.password": "<YOUR_PASSWORD>",
  "database.dbname": "<YOUR_DB_NAME>",
  "topic.prefix": "dbserver1",
  "snapshot.mode": "initial",
  "snapshot.max.threads": "2",
  "table.include.list": "public.table_with_pk.1#2/3"
}
```

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

* Database: PostgreSQL (any supported version)
* The table (or schema) name requires SQL quoting — e.g. it contains special characters, dots, `#`, `/`, a leading digit, uppercase, or is a reserved word.

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

With `snapshot.max.threads > 1`, Debezium 3.5 uses the new chunked/parallel snapshot algorithm. The initial snapshot should complete successfully regardless of whether the schema/table identifiers require quoting, exactly as the non-chunked snapshot (and Debezium 3.4) does.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

The connector immediately fails during the snapshot phase with a PostgreSQL syntax error:

```
Caused by: org.postgresql.util.PSQLException: ERROR: syntax error at or near ".1"
  Position: 42
    at ...RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.rowCountForTableChunked(RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.java:1093)
```

Root cause: the chunked snapshot path computes a per-table row count in `RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.rowCountForTableChunked`:

```java
"SELECT COUNT(1) FROM %s".formatted(jdbcConnection.getQualifiedTableName(tableId))
```

`JdbcConnection.getQualifiedTableName()` returns `schema + "." + table` **without quoting**. For a table such as `table_with_pk.1#2/3` this produces:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(1) FROM public.table_with_pk.1#2/3
```

which is invalid SQL — Postgres reports the syntax error at position 42 (the `.1`). The rest of the snapshot SQL quotes identifiers correctly via `quotedTableIdString(tableId)` / `TableId.toDoubleQuotedString()`; only `rowCountForTableChunked` uses the unquoted form. This code path is new in 3.5 (it only runs for the chunked algorithm, i.e. when `snapshot.max.threads > 1` and `legacy.snapshot.max.threads` is not enabled), which is why 3.4 was unaffected.

Note: `JdbcConnection.getQualifiedTableName()` is also used by `ReselectColumnsPostProcessor` as an identifier/map key (not SQL), so the fix belongs at the `rowCountForTableChunked` call site rather than in `getQualifiedTableName` itself.

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

Yes, reproduced on 3.5.2.Final. The offending line is present on `main` as well.

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

```log
ERROR [io.debezium.pipeline.ErrorHandler] The maximum number of 0 retries has been attempted
ERROR [io.debezium.embedded.async.AsyncEmbeddedEngine] Engine has failed with : java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException: org.apache.kafka.connect.errors.ConnectException: An exception occurred in the change event producer. This connector will be stopped.
...
Caused by: io.debezium.DebeziumException: org.postgresql.util.PSQLException: ERROR: syntax error at or near ".1"
  Position: 42
    at io.debezium.pipeline.source.AbstractSnapshotChangeEventSource.execute(AbstractSnapshotChangeEventSource.java:111)
    ...
Caused by: org.postgresql.util.PSQLException: ERROR: syntax error at or near ".1"
  Position: 42
    at io.debezium.relational.RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.rowCountForTableChunked(RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.java:1093)
    at io.debezium.relational.RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.lambda$createChunkedDataEvents$7(RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.java:572)
    at io.debezium.relational.RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.prepareTables(...)
    at io.debezium.relational.RelationalSnapshotChangeEventSource.createChunkedDataEvents(...)
```

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our tutorial deployment?**

Create a table whose name requires quoting and run a snapshot with `snapshot.max.threads=2`:

```sql
-- A primary-keyed table whose name requires quoting
CREATE TABLE public."table_with_pk.1#2/3" (
    id numeric(9,0) PRIMARY KEY,
    data varchar(50)
);
INSERT INTO public."table_with_pk.1#2/3" (id, data) VALUES (1, 'a'), (2, 'b');
```

Start a PostgreSQL connector with `snapshot.max.threads=2` capturing that table. The connector stops during the snapshot with `ERROR: syntax error at or near ".1"`.

**Workaround:** set the internal flag `legacy.snapshot.max.threads=true`, which keeps multi-threaded snapshotting via the legacy table-per-thread algorithm (which quotes identifiers correctly), or set `snapshot.max.threads=1`.
author: mly-zju

project_fields.Title: Chunked snapshot fails with SQL syntax error for tables whose names require quoting (rowCountForTableChunked uses unquoted identifier)

title: Build Week 5: Complete Platform Subsystem - signals, config management, and environment switching
body:
This GSoC 2026 project task completes the Platform Subsystem of the Debezium JBang CLI, building on the pipeline, source, destination, connection, transform, catalog, and pipeline log commands implemented in Weeks 1–4.

The following features remain unimplemented from the design document (DDD-40).

---

## Part 1: `debezium pipeline signal`

**Proposed Command:**
- `debezium pipeline signal <id> --type <type>` - send a signal to a running pipeline (e.g. stop snapshot, force re-read)

**Implementation:**
- New `PipelineSignal.java` command under `commands/pipeline/`
- `POST /api/pipelines/{id}/signals` via `PipelineService`
- Signal type DTO + JBang integration test

---

## Part 2: `debezium source verify-signals`

**Proposed Command:**
- `debezium source verify-signals <id>` - verify that signaling is correctly configured for a source

**Implementation:**
- New `SourceVerifySignals.java` command under `commands/source/`
- `GET /api/sources/{id}/verify-signals` via `SourceService`
- JBang integration test

---

## Part 3: CLI config management (`debezium config set` / `debezium config get`)

**Proposed Commands:**
- `debezium config set platform-url http://localhost:8080` - write the platform URL to `~/.dbz/config.yaml`
- `debezium config get` - print the current `~/.dbz/config.yaml`

**Implementation:**
- New top-level `ConfigCommand.java` with `set` and `get` subcommands
- Reads/writes `~/.dbz/config.yaml` via `ConfigUtil`
- Unit tests for config read/write

---

## Part 4: Multi-environment switching (`debezium switch`)

Currently the CLI reads a single platform URL from `~/.dbz/config.yaml`. Users working across dev/staging/production must pass `--platform-address` on every command (surfaced in community feedback). The goal is a `kubectl`-like experience: define all environments once, switch with a single command.

**Extended `~/.dbz/config.yaml` schema:**
```yaml
environments:
  dev:
    url: http://localhost:8080
  staging:
    url: http://staging-server:8080
  prod:
    url: http://prod-server:8080
default: dev
```


**Proposed Command:**

* `debezium switch <env>` - set the active environment; all subsequent commands use that environment's URL without `--platform-address`

**Implementation:**

* Extend `ConfigUtil` to parse the `environments`/`default` schema (backward-compatible with the existing single-URL format)
* `debezium switch <env>` writes the updated `default` key back to `~/.dbz/config.yaml`
* Update `PlatformFactory.resolveApiUrl()` to resolve the active environment's URL (`--platform-address` remains a per-command override)
* Unit tests covering the new schema and switch behavior; JBang integration test

---

## Part 5: `debezium version`

**Proposed Command:**

* `debezium version` - print the CLI version and the Platform API version it is connected to

**Implementation:**

* New top-level `VersionCommand.java`
* Reads CLI build version from the JAR manifest; optionally calls `GET /api/version` on the Platform if `--platform-address` or config is set

---

All features follow the established per-feature package layout (`commands.<feature>`, `platform.<feature>.{dto,mapper,service,api}`) and the `jbang/*JB` + `nativebuild/*IT` test split. After this issue is resolved, the Platform Subsystem is feature-complete per DDD-40 and Week 6 will begin the Build Subsystem (`debezium init`, `debezium build`, `debezium build --image`, `debezium push`).
author: Binayak490-cyber

project_fields.Title: Build Week 5: Complete Platform Subsystem - signals, config management, and environment switching

title: JDBC sink: optionally fail fast on startup when schema.evolution=none and target table is missing
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

### Summary

Add an optional startup validation mode for the Debezium JDBC sink connector so that it can fail fast when `schema.evolution=none` and the target table is missing.

### Motivation

The JDBC sink connector currently validates target table existence lazily, when the first record for a target table is processed.

This behavior works well when `schema.evolution=basic`, because the connector can create or alter the destination table based on the incoming record schema.

However, when `schema.evolution=none`, the connector is configured not to perform DDL schema evolution. In this mode, the target table is expected to be managed externally and already exist before the connector starts processing DML events.

If the target table is missing, the connector can still start successfully and remain in a `RUNNING` state until the first event arrives. For low-traffic topics, this can delay detection of a deployment or configuration problem for a long time.

### Expected behavior

Provide an optional configuration that validates target table existence during task startup when:

- `schema.evolution=none`
- the target table name can be resolved at startup
- startup validation is explicitly enabled

If the target table does not exist, the task should fail during startup with a clear error message.

### Current behavior

The connector starts successfully even if the target table is missing. The missing table is detected only later, when the first sink record is processed and the connector attempts to check or use the target table.

### Example use case

In some production environments, destination tables are created and evolved by an external DDL pipeline. The JDBC sink connector is responsible only for applying DML events.

In this setup, `schema.evolution=none` is intentional. A missing target table usually indicates that the DDL pipeline failed, the connector was deployed with the wrong `collection.name.format`, or the connector points to the wrong database/schema.

Failing fast during connector startup would make this problem visible immediately during deployment, instead of waiting until the next CDC event is consumed.

This is especially useful for low-traffic tables where the first event may arrive minutes or hours after connector deployment.

### Proposed configuration

One possible option:

```properties
table.validation.fail.fast=false
```

When set to `true`, the connector validates statically resolvable target tables during task startup.

The default should remain `false` for backward compatibility.

### Compatibility considerations

This should not change the default behavior.

Startup validation should not run for `schema.evolution=basic`, because a missing target table can be valid and expected in that mode.

Startup validation should only run when the target table name can be resolved without consuming a record. For configurations that depend on record contents, dynamic naming strategies, or values from the source block, the connector could either skip startup validation with a warning or reject the configuration when fail-fast validation is explicitly enabled.
author: minleejae

project_fields.Title: JDBC sink: optionally fail fast on startup when schema.evolution=none and target table is missing

title: JDBC Sink: Add CockroachDB integration tests for the CockroachDB dialect
body:
Follow-up to #1639 (merged in debezium/debezium#7570), as requested in https://github.com/debezium/dbz/issues/1639#issuecomment-4874195760.

The CockroachDB dialect shipped with unit tests only. Add integration tests running against a CockroachDB Testcontainers instance, mirroring the PostgreSQL suite where applicable:

- Column type mapping
- Insert modes (insert, upsert, update)
- Delete enabled (deletes, tombstones, truncate)
- Transaction conflict behavior: an induced serialization conflict (SQLSTATE 40001) recovers under the default retry settings, and fails fast with retries disabled (flush.max.retries=0)

Requires adding CockroachDB test infrastructure to debezium-connector-jdbc: the testcontainers-cockroachdb dependency, a COCKROACHDB SinkType, a CockroachDbSinkDatabaseContextProvider, an it-cockroachdb test tag, and a CI matrix entry.

title: JDBC Sink: CockroachDB dialect fails to write string arrays
body:
Problem:
Writing a record with a string array field (for example into a text[] column) through the CockroachDB dialect fails with:

org.postgresql.util.PSQLException: Unable to find server array type for provided name string.

The sink's ArrayType resolves the array element type name via the dialect, and the Hibernate CockroachDialect names character types 'string' (a CockroachDB alias). That name is valid in DDL but does not exist in pg_type, so the PgJDBC driver cannot resolve it in Connection.createArrayOf and the flush fails. Non-string arrays (int4[], bool[]) are unaffected.

Found by the CockroachDB integration tests added for #2180 (testShouldWorkWithTextArray).

Change:
Override the character JDBC type names in CockroachDBDatabaseDialect to the PostgreSQL-compatible name (text), which CockroachDB supports in both DDL and pg_type, so array binds resolve.

Verification:
testShouldWorkWithTextArray in the CockroachDB integration test suite passes; the remaining suite is unaffected.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: JDBC Sink: CockroachDB dialect fails to write string arrays

title: Oracle Connector: Missing CDC events
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

`Debezium 3.4.0`

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

selected connector settings:
```
"connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.oracle.OracleConnector",
"key.converter": "io.confluent.connect.avro.AvroConverter",
"key.converter.schema.registry.url": "[removed]",
"value.converter": "io.confluent.connect.avro.AvroConverter",
"value.converter.schema.registry.url": "[removed]",
"schema.name.adjustment.mode": "avro",
"database.query.timeout.ms": 0,
"log.mining.strategy": "hybrid",
"log.mining.transaction.retention.ms": 50000000,
"log.mining.query.filter.mode": "in",
"log.mining.batch.size.max": 200000,
"log.mining.batch.size.min": 40000,
"log.mining.batch.size.default": 40000,
"internal.log.mining.log.query.max.retries": "10",
"internal.log.mining.sql.relaxed.quote.detection": "true",
"lob.enabled": "false",
"unavailable.value.placeholder": "previous_value_unchanged",
"time.precision.mode": "connect",
"tasks.max": "1",
"post.processors": "reselector",
"reselector.type": "io.debezium.processors.reselect.ReselectColumnsPostProcessor",
"reselector.reselect.columns.include.list": "[removed]",
"reselector.reselect.unavailable.values": "true",
"reselector.reselect.null.values": "true"
```

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

`Oracle 19c` on premise

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

All events are produced to the Kafka topic.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

Some events are missing in the Kafka topic. The missing events are really rare, depending on the table, one minssing in half a million events, or rarer.

I suspect that it could be related to the reselector, because only tables on the reselector include list are affected. We have VARCHAR2 columns with extended string size on our database, therefore a lot of tables / columns are on the reselector include list.

This is what one specific transaction looked like:
START
UPDATE: Old row was updated. This was captured by the connector and produced to the Kafka topic.
INTERNAL
INSERT: New row was inserted. Extended VARCHAR2 columns were inserted with LM_EMPTY_STRING. This was not produced to the Kafka topic.
UPDATE: New row was updated. Extended VARCHAR2 columns were updated to NULL. This was not produced to the Kafka topic.
COMMIT

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

Not applicable. We are not using the latest version.

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

I have access to the logs, but there is nothing suspicious in them, only commits. We don't have DEBUG/TRACE because of the amount it generates.

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**

I have not tried to reproduce it.
author: hj9-dwh

project_fields.Title: Oracle Connector: Missing CDC events

The e2e pipeline suites (JdbcSinkPipelineToPostgresIT and siblings) run a Debezium source connector through Kafka into the JDBC sink against a target database, driven by per-type expectations in AbstractJdbcSinkPipelineIT. Add a CockroachDB target:

- JdbcSinkPipelineToCockroachDbIT tagged e2e-cockroachdb

title: ZeroDateFallbackConverter truncates DATETIME(4-6) to millisecond precision
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

MySQL and MariaDB connectors, 3.6.0-SNAPSHOT (main). The behavior was introduced in 3.6.0 with PR #7415 (DBZ-1912), which added `ZeroDateFallbackConverter`.

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

The issue is triggered whenever `ZeroDateFallbackConverter` is enabled and applies to a high-precision `DATETIME` column, e.g.:

```
converters=zerodate
zerodate.type=io.debezium.connector.binlog.converters.ZeroDateFallbackConverter
zerodate.target.types=DATETIME
```

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

MySQL 8.0 (also reproducible with MariaDB), on-premises. The problem is not environment-specific; it happens on any deployment where the converter is enabled on a `DATETIME(4-6)` column.

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

For a `DATETIME(4-6)` column, the emitted field should preserve microsecond precision, matching the normal Debezium converter path: the field schema should be `io.debezium.time.MicroTimestamp` and the value should be epoch-microseconds.

For example, a value of `2024-06-15 12:34:56.123456` should be emitted as `1718454896123456` with the `io.debezium.time.MicroTimestamp` schema.

The normal converter path in `JdbcValueConverters` dispatches on `getTimePrecision(column)`: precision 0-3 → `Timestamp` (millis), precision 4-6 → `MicroTimestamp` (micros). The converter output should be consistent with this.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

`ZeroDateFallbackConverter` always emits the `io.debezium.time.Timestamp` (milliseconds) schema regardless of the column's fractional-second precision, so `DATETIME(4-6)` columns are silently truncated to milliseconds.

For the same value `2024-06-15 12:34:56.123456`, the emitted value is `1718454896123` with the `io.debezium.time.Timestamp` schema, dropping the sub-millisecond precision.

Root cause: in `ZeroDateFallbackConverter.registerDatetime()`, the schema name and value conversion are both hardcoded to milliseconds:

```java
// always millis, regardless of field.length()
SchemaBuilder schema = SchemaBuilder.int64()
        .name(io.debezium.time.Timestamp.SCHEMA_NAME)   // should be MicroTimestamp for precision >= 4
        .version(SCHEMA_VERSION);
...
return ldt.toInstant(ZoneOffset.UTC).toEpochMilli();    // should be toEpochMicros for precision >= 4
```

Because the converter registers itself as a `CustomConverter` and replaces the normal schema/converter for that column entirely, its hardcoded millis schema takes effect for all `DATETIME` columns, including high-precision ones.

Proposed fix: in `registerDatetime()`, read `field.length()` and, when precision >= 4, use `io.debezium.time.MicroTimestamp.SCHEMA_NAME` for the schema and `Conversions.toEpochMicros()` for the fallback value and converter lambda. MySQL/MariaDB cap `DATETIME` precision at 6, so no nanosecond mapping is needed.

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

Yes. The affected code (`ZeroDateFallbackConverter`) was introduced in 3.6.0 via PR #7415 and is present on current main.

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

Not applicable. This is a schema/value mapping defect, not a runtime failure. It is confirmed by unit tests against `ZeroDateFallbackConverter` and by the emitted field schema/value for a `DATETIME(4-6)` column.

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**

1. Deploy a MySQL connector with `ZeroDateFallbackConverter` enabled for `DATETIME` (see configuration above).
2. Create a table with a `DATETIME(6)` column that allows zero-dates.
3. Insert a row with a non-zero datetime value, e.g. `2024-06-15 12:34:56.123456`.
4. Inspect the emitted Kafka Connect record: the field schema is `io.debezium.time.Timestamp` and the value is `1718454896123` (milliseconds), instead of `io.debezium.time.MicroTimestamp` / `1718454896123456` (microseconds).

---

## Related links

- PR #7415 — introduced `ZeroDateFallbackConverter`: https://github.com/debezium/debezium/pull/7415
- DBZ-1912 — zero-date fallback customization issue: https://github.com/debezium/dbz/issues/1912
author: skdus531

project_fields.Title: ZeroDateFallbackConverter truncates DATETIME(4-6) to millisecond precision

title: RocketMqConfig#toString() leaks the RocketMQ ACL secret key in clear text
body:
`RocketMqConfig#toString()` renders the RocketMQ ACL `secretKey` in clear text:

```
", secretKey='" + secretKey + '\'' +
```

The RocketMQ schema history storage (`RocketMqSchemaHistory`) builds this object from the `schema.history.internal.rocketmq.secret.key` property, so any log line, exception message, or debugger that prints the configuration would leak the credential.

Debezium already masks sensitive values elsewhere with the standard `"********"` placeholder (`Configuration#withMaskedPasswords`), so this appears to be an oversight rather than intended behavior. The access key is an access identifier (not a secret) and can remain visible for troubleshooting.
author: TimurRakhmatullin86

project_fields.Title: RocketMqConfig#toString() leaks the RocketMQ ACL secret key in clear text

title: MySQL Source Connector: map columns declared as BOOLEAN to INT16 by default, consistently with the snapshot phase
body:
## Feature request or enhancement
 A column declared as `BOOLEAN` is mapped differently depending on how its schema reaches the connector:

 - During snapshots the schema comes from `SHOW CREATE TABLE`, which returns `tinyint(1)`, so the field is emitted as `INT16` with values `0`/`1`.
 - A `CREATE`/`ALTER` statement parsed during streaming carries the original `BOOLEAN` keyword, so the same declaration produces a Kafka `boolean` field with `true`/`false` (`MySqlAntlrDdlParser` maps `BOOL`/`BOOLEAN` to
 `Types.BOOLEAN`, which `JdbcValueConverters` turns into `SchemaBuilder.bool()`).

 Both representations survive restarts, because the schema history replays each table's original text, so a single pipeline can permanently contain both encodings for identically declared columns. The behavior is described
 in the "Boolean values" section of the documentation, with the `TinyIntOneToBooleanConverter` as the workaround.

 I would like to propose making `INT16` the default for the streaming path as well:

 - Consistency is only achievable in this direction: `SHOW CREATE TABLE` cannot distinguish `BOOLEAN` from `TINYINT(1)`, and the `TINYINT(1)` length heuristic no longer works for e.g. `TINYINT(1) UNSIGNED` on MySQL 8.0.19+.
 - MySQL itself rewrites `BOOLEAN` to `TINYINT(1)` and does not enforce boolean semantics (the column can store any value from -128 to 127); the streaming path silently coerces such values to `true` today without the user
 opting in.
 - Users who want boolean semantics already have the opt-in `TinyIntOneToBooleanConverter` (DBZ-1801), which applies uniformly to both phases.

 Since this changes the emitted schema for streaming-created `BOOLEAN` columns (`boolean` -> `int16`), it would have to be gated on a major release or introduced behind an option. I am happy to work on a PR if the direction
 is agreed on.
author: minleejae

project_fields.Title: MySQL Source Connector: map columns declared as BOOLEAN to INT16 by default, consistently with the snapshot phase

title: Vitess connector drops BIT columns — VitessType does not resolve BIT, resolveValue returns null for Types.BIT
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

Vitess connector, 3.2.1.Final (and main). The affected code paths are present across all current releases.

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

Any configuration capturing a table with a `BIT` column.

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

Vitess / MySQL, any deployment.

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

`BIT(1)` columns should be emitted as boolean values (schema `BOOLEAN`); `BIT(N)` columns should be emitted as a byte array (schema `BYTES`). This matches the behaviour of the MySQL JDBC connector and the existing `JdbcValueConverters.convertBits` implementation, which already handles `Types.BIT` correctly.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

`BIT` columns are silently dropped from the change event. No value — and no field — appears in the record for any `BIT(N)` column.

Two separate gaps combine to produce the drop:

1. **`VitessType.resolve()` has no `"BIT"` case.**
   The switch in `VitessType.java` does not handle the MySQL type name `"BIT"`. It falls through to the `default` branch, which assigns JDBC type `1111` (unknown). A column typed as unknown is not carried forward.

2. **`ReplicationMessageColumnValueResolver.resolveValue()` has no case for `Types.BIT` (`-7`).**
   The switch that maps JDBC types to column values has no arm for `-7`. It falls through to the default `return null`, silently dropping the value even when the JDBC type were otherwise correct.

VStream already emits `BIT` column values as raw bytes via `asBytes()`, so no VStream-side changes are needed. The `JdbcValueConverters.convertBits` method already converts `Types.BIT` to the correct Kafka Connect type (boolean for `BIT(1)`, byte array for `BIT(N >= 2)`).

---

**Did you check the logs for potential problems?**

Yes. With a `BIT` column present, vtctld (Vitess) logs:

```
ERROR: unknown type BIT
```

On the connector side the column is absent from the emitted record with no connector-level error — the drop is silent.

---

**How to reproduce**

1. Create a MySQL table via Vitess with a `BIT` column, e.g.:
   ```sql
   CREATE TABLE t (id INT PRIMARY KEY, flag BIT(1), bits BIT(8));
   ```
2. Configure the Vitess connector against the keyspace containing this table.
3. Insert a row.
4. Observe that the change event for the row contains no `flag` or `bits` field.

---

**Fix**

Two one-line additions, no new dependencies:

**`VitessType.java` — `resolve()` switch:**
```java
case "BIT":
    return new VitessType("BIT", Types.BIT);
```

**`ReplicationMessageColumnValueResolver.java` — `resolveValue()` switch:**
```java
case Types.BIT:
    return columnValue.asBytes();
```

`JdbcValueConverters.convertBits` handles the `Types.BIT → BOOLEAN / BYTES` conversion correctly and needs no changes.

I'm happy to open a PR with the fix and tests (covering `BIT(1) → boolean` and `BIT(N) → bytes`).
author: mcrauwel

project_fields.Title: Vitess connector drops BIT columns — VitessType does not resolve BIT, resolveValue returns null for Types.BIT

title: JDBC Sink: CockroachDB dialect emits PostgreSQL type names that CockroachDB does not support
body:
Problem:
The CockroachDB dialect inherits all PostgreSQL type registrations, and several of them emit DDL that CockroachDB rejects. Observed in the end-to-end pipeline tests for #2185 with a PostgreSQL source, and verified directly against CockroachDB v25.4.12 and v26.3.0-alpha.1 (both behave identically):

- Unsupported type names emitted by the inherited mappings: xml, hstore (MAP), int4range, int8range, numrange, tsrange, tstzrange, daterange, macaddr, cidr, money, point, halfvec (FloatVector), sparsevec (SparseDoubleVector)
- Geometry and geography fields fail with: type "postgis.geography" does not exist. CockroachDB provides geometry, geography, and ST_GeomFromWKB natively, without a PostGIS extension schema
- Types verified as natively supported and correctly inherited: ltree, citext, inet, oid, tsvector, vector, bit, varbit, enum, interval, uuid, json/jsonb

Change:
Register CockroachDB-compatible overrides in CockroachDBDatabaseDialect: XML, ranges, macaddr, and cidr map to text; MAP maps to jsonb; money maps to decimal(19,2); geometry, geography, and point use the built-in spatial types with unqualified names and ST_GeomFromWKB; FloatVector maps to vector; SparseDoubleVector reports the standard unsupported-schema-type error since CockroachDB has no sparse vector type.

Verification:
The e2e-cockroachdb pipeline tests (#2185) cover these types against a real CockroachDB instance.
author: viragtripathi

project_fields.Title: JDBC Sink: CockroachDB dialect emits PostgreSQL type names that CockroachDB does not support
