title: PostgreSQL: snapshot reports serial/bigserial column type name while streaming reports int4/int8
body:
## Summary

For a PostgreSQL `serial` / `smallserial` / `bigserial` column, the reported `column.type.name` for the same column can differ between the snapshot and streaming paths, and the streaming value is decoder-dependent:

- **Snapshot** (`PostgresConnection#doReadTableColumn`) takes the type name from the JDBC driver metadata, which reports `serial` / `smallserial` / `bigserial`.
- **Streaming** typically derives the name from the column OID via `PostgresType#getName()`, i.e. the underlying `int4` / `int2` / `int8`. The value is not uniform across decoders, though — `OpenLineageIT#shouldProduceOpenLineageInputDatasetUponDDLEvent` currently expects `int4` for the pgoutput decoder but `serial` for the others on a DDL-refreshed column.

A custom converter (or any downstream consumer) matching on `column.type.name` therefore sees `serial` during snapshot but `int4` during streaming for the same column.

## Why this wasn't folded into the UDT fix

This surfaced while narrowing [debezium/debezium#7651](https://github.com/debezium/debezium/pull/7651) (fix for [#683](https://github.com/debezium/dbz/issues/683), unqualified UDT type names). That fix deliberately leaves `serial` untouched, because normalizing `serial → int4` in the snapshot path has a backward-compatibility cost:

- The type name flows into `__debezium.source.column.type` via `PropagateSourceMetadataToSchemaParameter`.
- The JDBC sink's `SerialType` relies on that value to re-create `SERIAL` / `BIGSERIAL` columns (`AbstractJdbcSinkPipelineIT.testSerialDataType` / `testSmallSerialDataType` / `testBigSerialDataType`). Collapsing `serial → int4` would make a PG→PG pipeline with `column.propagate.source.type` emit plain integer columns instead of `SERIAL`.

## Decision needed

Should snapshot and streaming agree on the type name for serial columns, and in which direction?

- Snapshot → `int4` (match streaming) is more internally consistent but drops the sink's ability to re-create `SERIAL` / `BIGSERIAL`.
- Keeping/deriving `serial` on the streaming side would preserve that ability, but the information isn't readily available from the replication stream OID (and is already decoder-inconsistent today).

Filing separately so the trade-off can be weighed on its own, independent of the UDT fix in [debezium/debezium#7651](https://github.com/debezium/debezium/pull/7651).
author: sundongkim-dev

project_fields.Title: PostgreSQL: snapshot reports serial/bigserial column type name while streaming reports int4/int8

title: Debezium Server 3.6.0 image is missing org.apache.avro
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

Debezium **Server 3.6.0.Final** (standalone), official image `quay.io/debezium/server:3.6.0`
(identical digest to `quay.io/debezium/server:3.6.0.Final` — `sha256:adec18409dff…`). Reproduced with
the **PostgreSQL** connector, but the failure is in the built-in **Avro format / Apicurio serde** and is
connector-agnostic (it occurs during converter initialization, before the source connector starts).

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

Debezium Server `application.properties`:

```properties
debezium.sink.type=kafka
debezium.sink.kafka.producer.bootstrap.servers=redpanda:9092
debezium.sink.kafka.producer.key.serializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.ByteArraySerializer
debezium.sink.kafka.producer.value.serializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.ByteArraySerializer

debezium.source.connector.class=io.debezium.connector.postgresql.PostgresConnector
debezium.source.offset.storage.file.filename=/debezium/data/offsets.dat
debezium.source.offset.flush.interval.ms=60000
debezium.source.database.hostname=postgres
debezium.source.database.port=5432
debezium.source.database.user=postgres
debezium.source.database.password=postgres
debezium.source.database.dbname=postgres
debezium.source.topic.prefix=repro
debezium.source.plugin.name=pgoutput
debezium.source.slot.name=repro_slot
debezium.source.schema.include.list=public

# Built-in Avro format via the Apicurio serde.
# The registry URL is intentionally a non-existent host: it is never contacted,
# because the failure happens during converter init (class load), before any registry call.
debezium.format.key=avro
debezium.format.value=avro
debezium.format.key.apicurio.registry.url=http://dummy-registry:8080/apis/registry/v3
debezium.format.value.apicurio.registry.url=http://dummy-registry:8080/apis/registry/v3
debezium.format.key.apicurio.registry.auto-register=true
debezium.format.value.apicurio.registry.auto-register=true

quarkus.log.console.json=false
```

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

PostgreSQL 16 (`postgres:16-alpine`), running locally via Docker Compose. The database version/mode is
not material: the server fails at startup **before** any connection to the source database is attempted.

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

Debezium Server should start and serialize records using the Avro format out of the box with the official
image — i.e. `org.apache.avro:avro` should be present on the server runtime classpath (`/debezium/lib`),
as it is in the 3.4.x images.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

The server fails to start. Constructing the Apicurio `AvroKafkaSerializer` throws
`NoClassDefFoundError: org/apache/avro/io/Encoder`, because Apache Avro is not on the classpath.

Classpath inspection of `quay.io/debezium/server:3.6.0`:
```
$ docker run --rm --entrypoint sh quay.io/debezium/server:3.6.0 -c 'ls /debezium/lib | grep -iE "avro|apicurio-registry-(avro|utils)"'
apicurio-registry-avro-serde-kafka-3.1.7.jar
apicurio-registry-serde-common-avro-3.1.7.jar     # AvroKafkaSerializer lives here; requires org.apache.avro
apicurio-registry-utils-converter-3.2.4.jar
# -> no avro-*.jar present in /debezium/lib
```
The only `avro-*.jar` anywhere in the 3.6.0 image is `avro-1.11.3.jar` bundled **inside the Cassandra/DSE
connector directories** (`/debezium/connectors/debezium-connector-cassandra-*/`, `.../dse/`), which are
not on the Debezium Server classpath.

For contrast, `quay.io/debezium/server:3.4.3` **does** ship it in `/debezium/lib`:
```
avro-1.12.0.jar
apicurio-registry-serdes-avro-serde-2.6.13.Final.jar
```

Likely contributing factors:
- The Apicurio 3.1.x Avro serde (`apicurio-registry-serde-common-avro`) declares `org.apache.avro:avro`
  at (default) **compile** scope, so it should be included transitively.
- **Version skew** in the bundled Apicurio jars: serde jars are `3.1.7` while
  `apicurio-registry-utils-converter` (and others) are `3.2.4`.
- `debezium-server-bom` still declares dependency management for the **pre-3.x** Apicurio coordinates
  (`apicurio-registry-serdes-avro-serde`, `apicurio-registry-serdes-jsonschema-serde`,
  `apicurio-registry-client`), which no longer exist under Apicurio 3.x.

Appears to be a regression from the Apicurio 2.6 → 3.x upgrade (DBZ-9538).

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

Yes. 3.6.0.Final is the latest released version, and it reproduces (both `:3.6.0` and `:3.6.0.Final` are
the same image). The `debezium-server-bom` on `main` still references the pre-3.x Apicurio coordinates, so
current snapshots appear affected as well (not verified against a 3.7 Alpha/Beta build).

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

The container exits at startup. Failure excerpt:
```
ERROR [io.quarkus.runtime.Application] (main) Failed to start application: java.lang.RuntimeException: Failed to start quarkus
Caused by: java.lang.NoClassDefFoundError: org/apache/avro/io/Encoder
    at io.apicurio.registry.serde.avro.AvroKafkaSerializer.<init>(AvroKafkaSerializer.java:20)
    at io.apicurio.registry.utils.converter.AvroConverter.configure(AvroConverter.java:50)
    at io.debezium.embedded.ConverterBuilder.createConverter(ConverterBuilder.java:243)
    at io.debezium.embedded.ConverterBuilder.toFormat(ConverterBuilder.java:95)
    at io.debezium.embedded.async.AsyncEmbeddedEngine$AsyncEngineBuilder.build(AsyncEmbeddedEngine.java:1082)
    at io.debezium.server.DebeziumServer.start(DebeziumServer.java:162)
    ...
Caused by: java.lang.ClassNotFoundException: org.apache.avro.io.Encoder
```

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**

The tutorial deployment is Kafka Connect–based, so it does not exercise the Debezium **Server** image
where this bug lives. Below is an equivalent **minimal standalone Debezium Server** Docker Compose that
reproduces it (using the `application.properties` above):

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
    command: ["postgres","-c","wal_level=logical","-c","max_replication_slots=4","-c","max_wal_senders=4"]

  redpanda:
    image: redpandadata/redpanda:v24.2.7
    command:
      - redpanda
      - start
      - --mode=dev-container
      - --smp=1
      - --kafka-addr=PLAINTEXT://0.0.0.0:9092
      - --advertise-kafka-addr=PLAINTEXT://redpanda:9092

  debezium-server:
    image: quay.io/debezium/server:3.6.0
    depends_on: [postgres, redpanda]
    restart: "no"
    volumes:
      - ./application.properties:/debezium/config/application.properties:ro
```

```bash
docker compose up
```

The `debezium-server` container fails at startup with the `NoClassDefFoundError` above.

**Workaround:** add a compatible `org.apache.avro:avro` (1.11.x/1.12.x) to `/debezium/lib`, e.g. layer it
into a custom image (reusing the jar already present in the image):
```dockerfile
FROM quay.io/debezium/server:3.6.0
USER root
RUN cp /debezium/connectors/debezium-connector-cassandra-5/avro-1.11.3.jar /debezium/lib/
USER 185
```
author: data-plumber-gk

title: Oracle Connector fails on unparseable DDL despite skip.unparseable.ddl=true
body:
Oracle Connector fails on unparseable DDL despite skip.unparseable.ddl=true
Environment
•	Debezium Oracle Connector: 3.6.0.CR1
•	Kafka Connect Runtime: 4.2.0
•	Oracle Database: Oracle PDB
•	Capture mode: LogMiner (log.mining.strategy=online_catalog)
Connector configuration
Relevant settings:
JSON
{
"connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.oracle.OracleConnector",
"skip.unparseable.ddl": "true",
"snapshot.mode": "no_data",
"log.mining.strategy": "online_catalog",
"include.schema.changes": "false"
}

Problem Description
The connector stops with a ParsingException when processing an Oracle DDL statement.
The connector is configured with:
.properties
skip.unparseable.ddl=true

We expected the connector to log a warning and continue streaming changes, but instead the task transitions to FAILED.
Failing DDL

ALTER TABLE TBTIPO_TRATAMENTO_TRIBUTARIO
MODIFY SQ_TIPO GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY
(START WITH 22 INCREMENT BY 1) NOT NULL;

Error

io.debezium.text.ParsingException:
DDL statement couldn't be parsed

ALTER TABLE TBTIPO_TRATAMENTO_TRIBUTARIO
MODIFY SQ_TIPO GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY
(START WITH 22 INCREMENT BY 1) NOT NULL;

no viable alternative at input
'ALTER TABLE TBTIPO_TRATAMENTO_TRIBUTARIO MODIFY SQ_TIPO GENERATED'
Show more lines
Stack trace excerpt:

Caused by: io.debezium.text.ParsingException
at io.debezium.connector.oracle.OracleSchemaChangeEventEmitter.emitSchemaChangeEvent(...)
at io.debezium.pipeline.EventDispatcher.dispatchSchemaChangeEvent(...)
at io.debezium.connector.oracle.logminer.AbstractLogMinerStreamingChangeEventSource.dispatchSchemaChangeEventInternal(...)
...
Caused by: org.antlr.v4.runtime.NoViableAltException

Observed behavior
After the failure:
JSON
{
"connector": {
"state": "RUNNING"
},
"tasks": [
{
"id": 0,
"state": "FAILED"
}
]
}
Restarting the connector causes it to fail again at the same DDL event.
The connector offset is preserved and the failure is reproducible.
Additional Information
The affected table is not part of the connector's table.include.list.
Example:
.properties
table.include.list=REDE_SIMPLES.TBSOLICITACAO_RECEPCAO_RS

However, the connector still encounters and attempts to parse the DDL for:

TBTIPO_TRATAMENTO_TRIBUTARIO

during schema change processing.
Expected Behavior
When:
.properties
skip.unparseable.ddl=true

is configured, an unsupported or unparseable DDL statement should be skipped (or logged as a warning), allowing the connector to continue streaming changes.

Question
Is this a known limitation of the Oracle DDL parser regarding:

GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY

or is this a bug where skip.unparseable.ddl=true is not being honored during schema change event processing?
Thank you.
author: alexalves83

title: MySQL connector: map signed TINYINT to INT8, consistent with the smallest-container principle
body:
### Current behavior

The MySQL DDL parser folds `TINYINT` into `Types.SMALLINT` at registration (`MySqlAntlrDdlParser.java:182`), so both signed and unsigned TINYINT are emitted as Kafka `INT16`.

### Why this is inconsistent

Debezium's own mapping principle — stated in the unsigned-widening comments in `BinlogValueConverters.java:159-176` ("In order to capture unsigned SMALLINT 16-bit data source, INT32 will be required to safely capture all valid values") — is *the smallest signed Kafka type that safely holds the source range*. Every MySQL integer type follows it except TINYINT:

| MySQL type (range) | Current | Smallest container |
| --- | --- | --- |
| TINYINT (-128..127) | INT16 | **INT8** ← sole deviation |
| TINYINT UNSIGNED (0..255) | INT16 | INT16 ✓ (but only as a side effect of the folding — no explicit rule) |
| SMALLINT (-32768..32767) | INT16 | INT16 ✓ |
| SMALLINT UNSIGNED (0..65535) | INT32 | INT32 ✓ |
| MEDIUMINT / MEDIUMINT UNSIGNED | INT32 | INT32 ✓ (unsigned also implicit) |
| INT / INT UNSIGNED | INT32 / INT64 | ✓ |
| BIGINT / BIGINT UNSIGNED | INT64 / mode-based | ✓ |

Ranges per the MySQL Reference Manual, [13.1.2 Integer Types (Exact Value)](https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.4/en/integer-types.html). Note that `INT8` is already an established output type in Debezium — the Oracle connector emits it for `NUMBER(1..2)` (`OracleValueConverters.java:172`).

### Proposal

- Map signed `TINYINT` to `INT8`.
- Add explicit widening rules for `TINYINT UNSIGNED` → `INT16` (and `MEDIUMINT UNSIGNED` → `INT32`) alongside the existing SMALLINT/INT/BIGINT UNSIGNED rules, instead of relying on the parser folding.

### Compatibility assessment

- **Wire**: unchanged for Avro/JSON — `INT8`/`INT16` are both Avro `int` (zig-zag varint); only the `connect.type` annotation changes.
- **JDBC sink**: existing target columns are unaffected (value binding is `Number`-based; `schema.evolution` does not alter existing column types). Newly created columns become `TINYINT` instead of `SMALLINT` via `ConnectInt8Type` → `dialect.getTypeName(Types.TINYINT)` — arguably more faithful to the source. Dialects without a tiny integer type (e.g. PostgreSQL) resolve `Types.TINYINT` to `smallint`, same as today.
- **Consumers**: code that hard-casts the field value to `Short` would need to handle `Byte`. This is the one breaking surface; schema-driven consumers (Avro/JSON converters, Connect `Struct#get`) are unaffected.

Related: #2189 — if this lands, the consistent mapping target for columns declared as `BOOLEAN` (tinyint(1) alias) becomes `INT8` as well.
author: minleejae

project_fields.Title: MySQL connector: map signed TINYINT to INT8, consistent with the smallest-container principle

title: Incremental snapshot window open/close watermarks cannot be attributed to the requesting signal or data collection
body:
## Feature request or enhancement

**Which use case/requirement will be addressed by the proposed feature?**

The signal-table watermarks that bracket every incremental snapshot chunk (`snapshot-window-open` / `snapshot-window-close`) currently carry only a per-chunk UUID in the `id` column and open/close wall-clock timestamps in the `data` column (`SignalMetadata`). They carry neither the id of the `execute-snapshot` signal that requested the snapshot nor the data collection the chunk belongs to.

External tooling that consumes the signal table's CDC events to track snapshot progress — e.g. data-reconciliation / recovery pipelines that need to timebox a table's snapshot window precisely, using the source-commit timestamp (`source.ts_ms`) of the watermark events — therefore cannot attribute a window to a request or to a table. When one `execute-snapshot` signal lists several data collections, or when a second signal arrives while a snapshot is running (Debezium appends its collections to the same queue), all resulting watermarks are indistinguishable. Consumers are forced to serialize snapshot requests per connector and aggregate all windows into one opaque batch, which also makes it impossible to detect or ignore watermarks from an unrelated, concurrently triggered snapshot.

The notification channel (`IncrementalSnapshotNotificationService`) is not a substitute: its events carry the correlation id but are stamped with connector wall-clock time rather than the source-commit time that watermark CDC events carry, so they cannot be used to bound a snapshot window against the change stream.

The connector already has all the required information in memory at the point where the watermarks are written: `signalPayload.id` is stored as the context's `correlationId`, and the current data collection is known. It just isn't included in the watermark rows.

---

**Implementation ideas (optional)**

Enrich the watermark `SignalMetadata` JSON (the `data` column of the watermark rows, which nothing parses on the connector side — `OpenIncrementalSnapshotWindow` / `CloseIncrementalSnapshotWindow` key only off the `id` column, so the change is backward-compatible) with two fields:

* `correlationId` — the id of the `execute-snapshot` signal that requested the data collection being chunked;
* `dataCollectionId` — the fully-qualified identifier of that data collection.

To make the attribution correct when collections from *different* signals are queued in one context, the correlation id should be tracked per `DataCollection` rather than read from the context-level field (which is
overwritten by each arriving signal — the same effect can today mislabel notifications for the first signal's remaining collections):

1. `DataCollection` gains a `correlationId`, set in `addDataCollectionNamesToSnapshot` from the arriving signal's id, and round-tripped through the offsets (a new key in the data-collections JSON; offsets written by older versions deserialize with a null correlation id).
2. `SignalBasedIncrementalSnapshotChangeEventSource` captures the current collection's identifier and correlation id when it emits the window-open row and reuses the captured values for the matching window-close row (the collection queue may already have advanced to the next collection by the time the close of a table's last chunk is emitted).

Scope notes: applies to the `INSERT_INSERT` watermarking strategy (with `INSERT_DELETE` there is no close row to enrich); read-only incremental snapshots write no signal-table watermarks and are unaffected; `MongoDbIncrementalSnapshotContext` mirrors the per-collection correlation id for consistency, though the MongoDB watermark documents don't carry metadata today.

A patch implementing the above (with unit tests covering the metadata format, the offset round-trip, and the open/close attribution across queued signals) is ready to be submitted as a PR.
author: cuong-dang

project_fields.Title: Incremental snapshot window open/close watermarks cannot be attributed to the requesting signal or data collection

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

_E.g. on-premises, with a specific cloud provider, etc._

`AWS RDS`

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

`All the tables has been there and running with the connector version mysql-3.0.6 for a while now (years). But yesterday we updated it to version latest 3.6.0.Final since there were no breaking changes that affected us. It was running for a few hours but then it started failing with ParsingException. We havent made any recent DDL changes to these tables either but it is picking up old data from history topic I guess. We expect the DDL changes to be ignored as we dont use those. They are for history keeping when we updated to V3.0.6 .`

---

project_fields.Title: io.debezium.text.ParsingException after updating to debezium-connector-mysql-3.6.0.Final-plugin.tar

title: JDBC sink throws NullPointerException instead of a clear error for a PostgreSQL array column on a sink without array support (e.g. MySQL)
body:
## Bug report

**What Debezium connector do you use and what version?**

Debezium JDBC sink connector, 3.7.0-SNAPSHOT (current `main`).

---

**What is the connector configuration?**

JDBC sink targeting **MySQL**, auto-creating tables, fed from a PostgreSQL source that has an array column (e.g. `int[]` / `text[]`):

```json
{
  "connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.jdbc.JdbcSinkConnector",
  "connection.url": "jdbc:mysql://<host>:3306/<db>",
  "schema.evolution": "basic",
  "primary.key.mode": "record_key",
  "insert.mode": "upsert"
}
```

---

**What is the captured database version and mode of deployment?**

Source: PostgreSQL (any recent version) with an array-typed column. Sink: MySQL 8.x / 9.x. On-premises / containers.

---

**What behavior do you expect?**

Either the array column is mapped to a supported MySQL type, or — since MySQL has no native array type — the connector fails with a **clear, actionable error** stating that the column type cannot be resolved. It should not throw an opaque `NullPointerException`.

---

**What behavior do you see?**

The sink task fails while generating the `CREATE TABLE` statement with:

```
java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke "String.hashCode()" because "<local3>" is null
	at io.debezium.connector.jdbc.dialect.GeneralDatabaseDialect.getSchemaType(GeneralDatabaseDialect.java:490)
	at io.debezium.connector.jdbc.dialect.mysql.MySqlDatabaseDialect.getSchemaType(MySqlDatabaseDialect.java:141)
	at io.debezium.connector.jdbc.dialect.GeneralDatabaseDialect.lambda$getCreateTableStatement$0(GeneralDatabaseDialect.java:275)
	...
	at io.debezium.connector.jdbc.DefaultRecordWriter.createTable(DefaultRecordWriter.java:154)
```

**Root cause:** a Connect `ARRAY` schema has no logical name (`schema.name() == null`), and `ARRAY` is not registered in the MySQL dialect's type registry (`ArrayType` is registered only in `PostgresDatabaseDialect`). So `getSchemaType` falls through to `switch (schema.name())`, which the compiler lowers to `schema.name().hashCode()` → NPE. The `default ->` branch that was meant to throw the clear `ConnectException("Failed to resolve column type ...")` is never reached.

---

**Do you see the same behaviour using the latest released Debezium version?**

Yes — reproduces on current `main`. The relevant `switch` was last changed for the vector types and the `null` case has never been guarded.

---

**Do you have the connector logs, ideally from start till finish?**

The `NullPointerException` above is the terminal failure; the task then reports `JDBC sink connector failure` on the following put.

---

**How to reproduce the issue using our [tutorial](https://github.com/debezium/debezium-examples/tree/main/tutorial) deployment?**

Stream a PostgreSQL table with an array column (`CREATE TABLE t (id int primary key, tags text[])`) into a JDBC sink pointed at MySQL with `schema.evolution=basic`. The sink fails on table creation.

---

**Open question for maintainers:** is an explicit "cannot resolve column type" error the intended behavior for array columns on array-less sinks, or should the JDBC sink instead map arrays to a portable type such as `JSON`/text on those dialects? A fix for the opaque NPE (turning it into the intended clear error) is straightforward; the mapping decision is left to the maintainers.
author: sundongkim-dev

project_fields.Title: JDBC sink throws NullPointerException instead of a clear error for a PostgreSQL array column on a sink without array support (e.g. MySQL)

title: Build Week 7: Build Subsystem - debezium build and OCI image assembly
body:
**Description:**

Following Week 6 (`debezium init`, `dbz.yaml` schema, `debezium validate`), Week 7 implements the core of the Build Subsystem: the `debezium build` command. It reads the `dbz.yaml` produced by `debezium init`, resolves connector and Debezium Server dependencies using the embedded Maven Resolver API (no Maven installation required on the user machine), and assembles a trimmed server classpath directory. With `--image`, it wraps the result in a layered OCI container image using Jib Core API - no Docker daemon required.

---

## Part 1: Connector manifest registry

A YAML file bundled inside the CLI binary at compile time, mapping connector short names to Maven coordinates, supported versions and compatible sinks:

```yaml
connectors:
  postgres:
    artifact: io.debezium:debezium-connector-postgres
    versions: ["2.7.x", "3.0.x", "3.1.x"]
    sinks: [kafka, http, pulsar, redis]

  mysql:
    artifact: io.debezium:debezium-connector-mysql
    versions: ["2.7.x", "3.0.x", "3.1.x"]
    sinks: [kafka, http, pulsar, redis]

  mongodb:
    artifact: io.debezium:debezium-connector-mongodb
    versions: ["2.7.x", "3.0.x", "3.1.x"]
    sinks: [kafka, http, pulsar, redis]

  sqlserver:
    artifact: io.debezium:debezium-connector-sqlserver
    versions: ["2.7.x", "3.0.x", "3.1.x"]
    sinks: [kafka, http, pulsar, redis]

  oracle:
    artifact: io.debezium:debezium-connector-oracle
    versions: ["2.7.x", "3.0.x", "3.1.x"]
    sinks: [kafka, http, pulsar, redis]
```

**Implementation:**

- `ConnectorManifest.java` - record holding `artifact`, `versions`, `sinks`
- `ConnectorRegistry.java` - loads the bundled manifest YAML and resolves connector name → Maven coordinates
- Advanced users can override with a custom Maven coordinate directly in `dbz.yaml`
- Unit tests for manifest lookup and unknown connector error handling

---

## Part 2: `debezium build`

```bash
debezium build
debezium build --config path/to/dbz.yaml    # defaults to ./dbz.yaml
```

Reads `dbz.yaml`, looks up the connector in the manifest, uses the embedded Maven Resolver API (`maven-resolver-*`) to resolve the full transitive dependency graph for the connector + sink combination, and assembles the classpath as a **directory of JARs** under `target/debezium-server/` - no Maven installation required on the user machine.

**Implementation:**

- New `BuildCommand.java` under `commands/build/`
- `MavenResolver.java` - wraps Maven Resolver APIs (embedded, adds ~2MB to binary)
- Resolves `debezium-server-core` + connector artifact + their transitive runtime deps
- Output is a directory of JARs rather than a fat JAR - intentional for Jib layering (stable core JARs in lower layers, connector JARs in upper layers)
- Progress output: prints each resolved artifact as it downloads
- Integration tests with real Maven dependency resolution for a postgres-kafka pair

**Deliverable:** `debezium build` produces a working Debezium Server classpath directory for a given `dbz.yaml`

---

## Part 3: `debezium build --image`

```bash
debezium build --image
```

Extends `debezium build` to wrap the resolved JAR directory in a layered OCI container image using Jib Core API — no Docker daemon required. Image name, tag, and registry are read from the `build.image` block in `dbz.yaml`.

**4-layer image structure:**

| Layer | Contents | Cache behaviour |
|------|----------|-----------------|
| Layer 1 | Base JRE (`eclipse-temurin:21-jre`) | Stable, cached across all builds |
| Layer 2 | Debezium Core JARs | Cached as long as Debezium version unchanged |
| Layer 3 | Connector + Sink JARs | Changes only when connector/sink type or version changes |
| Layer 4 | Config + Entrypoint (`dbz.yaml`, `application.properties`, startup script) | Changes on every config edit |

This layering means `debezium push` on repeat builds only uploads the upper layers.

**Implementation:**

- `--image` flag on `BuildCommand`
- Jib Core API (`com.google.cloud.tools:jib-core`) for daemon-less OCI image assembly
- Image written to local OCI tarball (`target/debezium-server.tar`) ready for `docker load` or `debezium push`
- Image metadata: labels, entrypoint, environment variables from `dbz.yaml`
- Integration tests for image layer assembly

**Deliverable:** `debezium build --image` produces a runnable layered OCI image locally

---

All features follow the established per-feature package layout and the `jbang/*JB` + `nativebuild/*IT` test split. After this issue is resolved, Week 8 will implement `debezium push` - authenticating and pushing the assembled OCI image to Docker Hub, GHCR, or a custom registry.
author: Binayak490-cyber

project_fields.Title: Build Week 7: Build Subsystem - debezium build and OCI image assembly
